title: A numerical study of thermoelectric coupling in a photovoltaic-thermal system with a new heat exchanger compared to a photovoltaic module
authors: Hayat Allam, Moustafa Al-Damook, Yassine El Alami, Hicham El Achouby, Youness Bannour, Zineb Hekss, Charaf Hajjaj, Ahmed Faize, Bachir Benhala, Elhadi Baghaz
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study presents a comparative analysis of the performance of a conventional photovoltaic module (PVM) and a photovoltaic thermal system (PVTS) incorporating a new heat exchanger and water cooling. A 3D numerical model was developed to assess the impact of solar irradiance on the thermal and electrical performance of the two systems. The model was validated by comparison with previously published results, demonstrating good agreement and confirming its accuracy and reliability. The results show that the PVTS significantly reduces the temperature of the PV cells, with a maximum difference of $15.21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ compared to the uncooled PVM. This thermal reduction leads to improved electrical performance, with a decrease in efficiency limited to $0.66 \%$ for the PVTS, compared to $1.74 \%$ for the PVM, as well as higher electrical power reaching 221.67 W. In addition, the PVTS ensures efficient recovery of thermal energy, resulting in an increase in the fluid outlet temperature and an improvement in thermal efficiency of $11.78 \%$. These results confirm the value of the PVTS for the simultaneous recovery of electrical and thermal energy and highlight its potential for improving the overall energy efficiency of solar installations.

title: Adaptive Optimization of Circular Economy Strategies Under Uncertainty
authors: Abderrahman Mansouri, Bellat Abdelouahad, Idriss Bennis, Ali Siadat, Kawtar Ibn Batouta, Fatiha Akef
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Evaluating circular economy strategies in industrial systems is difficult because several objectives must be balanced while operating conditions remain uncertain. Many optimization models still rely on fixed assumptions, which makes their results less reliable when costs, environmental conditions, or operational parameters vary. This work develops an adaptive framework that combines scenario-based uncertainty modeling with multi-objective genetic optimization to study the robustness of circular strategies. Economic, environmental, circular, and operational dimensions are considered. For each scenario, Paretooptimal solutions are generated and compared through front dispersion, centroid displacement, and stability indicators. The simulations indicate that uncertainty changes both the shape and the position of trade-off structures, which also affects the ranking of candidate strategies. Overall, the framework helps evaluate circular economy options in a more robust way than deterministic formulations.

title: Multi Level Functional Connectivity Fusion Classification Framework for Brain Disease Diagnosis
authors: K Venkata Sai Saranya, A Prudhvi Raj, M Raja Kumari, Kolluru Swarnalatha, SK Samshad Begum, G Lakshmi Sowmya
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: Brain disease diagnosis has emerged as a key area in the intersection of artificial intelligence and neuroscience. Quantitative analysis of functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data provides biomarkers that aid clinical diagnosis, with functional connectivity (FC) analysis being a primary method. However, most existing studies focus on low-order FC features, overlooking the significance of high-order functional relationships among brain regions. To address this, we propose a novel multi-level FC fusion classification framework (MFC) for brain disease diagnosis. Our approach utilizes a deep neural network (DNN) to extract abstract feature representations from low-order and high-order FC patterns. The DNN training incorporates unsupervised and supervised learning, with prototype learning applied during supervised fine-tuning to enhance intra-class compactness and inter-class separability. The resulting multi-level FC features were combined using a hierarchical stacking strategy to train an ensemble classifier for brain disease classification. Experiments on large-scale fMRI datasets demonstrated robust performance across different preprocessing pipelines, brain parcellations, and cross-validation schemes. These findings highlight the efficacy and generality of the MFC model, providing a promising approach to integrate low-order and high-order FC patterns for improved brain disease classification.

title: Advancing FCEVs/FCHEVs: Overcoming Challenges for Scalable Zero-Emission Transport
authors: Debani Prasad Mishra, Prabin Kumar Parida, Rupam Samanta, Abhinav Singh, Kumar Pritam Maharana, Bibhu Prasad Nanda
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: The key to zero emission transportation and combating climate change is the change away from internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles and towards cleaner alternatives. Increasing greenhouse gas emissions, the depletion of fossil fuels, concerns for energy security have grown demand for new technologies including battery electric vehicles (BEVs), Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEVs) and Fuel Cell hybrid electric vehicles (FCHEVs). Although BEVs are presently the most popular vehicles, they still have certain disadvantages, for example a small range, demand for rare raw materials and lengthy charging times. However, hydrogen FCEVs and FCHEVs are great for heavy-duty and long distance transportation accompanied by their lengths driving ranges and speedy refueling.However, the cost of producing and storing hydrogen is the main issue. With an emphasis on green hydrogen generated by electrolysis using renewable energy sources, this study explores the potential of the hydrogen economy. It examines ways to reduce production costs, improve fuel cell performance, and make storage safer. It will be crucial to overcome these financial and technological obstacles in order to accelerate the transition away from internal combustion engines (ICE) and create a transportation system that is durable, effective, and completely emission-free.

title: A Context-Aware Heuristic for Meal Planning: Aggregation-Driven Filtering and Dynamic Weighted Sampling
authors: Khadija Salih Alj, Raifa Akkaoui
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Generating weekly meal plans is a combinatorial optimization problem balancing hard constraints (e.g., allergies) with soft objectives (e.g., ingredient reuse). This problem presents a critical latency-versus-optimality trade-off: global optimization solvers are too slow for real-time applications, while simple heuristics like Greedy Best-First are fast but suboptimal. This paper proposes a Context-Aware Heuristic that finds high-quality, near-optimal solutions within mobile-latency budgets. The two-phase architecture first uses AggregationDriven Filtering (a database-side pipeline) to satisfy hard constraints and prune the $\mathcal{N}$-recipe search space to a small, fixedsize candidate pool. Second, a Dynamic Weighted Sampling mechanism stochastically constructs the 7-day schedule, using a utility function that contextualizes taste similarity, ingredient reuse, and diversity based on prior selections. On a large dataset, CAH achieves 100% constraint satisfaction, significantly outperforms the Greedy baseline ($\mathbf{+ 5 4 \%}$ reuse, $\mathbf{+ 3 0 \%}$ diversity), and approximates 95% of a global Constraint Programming solver’s optimality score while being 122x faster. The heuristic is validated in a production mobile app, achieving a median latency of $\mathbf{1 4 7 ~ m s}$.

title: Extractive Summarization using TextRank with FastText Word Embedding and Cosine Similarity for News Summarization
authors: Subhan Subhan, Novita Ayu Indirawati, Yahya Nur Ifriza
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The rapid evolution of technology has fundamentally reshaped the landscape of digital news dissemination. However, the continuous surge in available data has precipitated a state of information overload, forcing readers to navigate vast quantities of content—a process that often undermines overall cognitive efficiency. Consequently, automated text summarization has emerged as a pivotal solution for optimizing the consumption of information. This research utilizes the TextRank algorithm, integrated with FastText word embeddings and cosine similarity, to transform textual input into vector representations. This methodology facilitates the ranking of sentences based on their thematic relevance, ensuring that the generated summaries remain both precise and concise. To validate the system’s efficacy, the ROUGE metric was employed to conduct a comparative analysis between the system-generated output and human reference summaries. Empirical results demonstrated high performance, with F-scores of 0.92, 0.87, and 0.92 for ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L, respectively. Optimal outcomes were achieved by configuring the system to a maximum of ten sentences and applying a similarity threshold of 0.4 to govern the connectivity within the sentence network. This specific calibration allowed for a sophisticated balance between brevity and comprehensiveness, yielding high-quality summaries that closely mirror the structural and substantive integrity of the reference texts.

title: Buckling Detection of Truss Members in Steel Bridges using Photogrammetric Principle with Computer Vision Technique
authors: Ayyah M. Baulo, Charles Julius D. Lozada, Chrissiel May M. Mamon, Rolly S. Tambe, Luke Mark B. Culili, Arianne R. Recto
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: The use of technology in detecting and monitoring bridges’ safety and health is a timely solution to the problems brought by defective structures to commuters. In this study, the researchers utilized MATLAB Programming and Internet-of-Things to develop an application to address this problem. The study aims to create an application that can detect buckled truss members of the steel bridge when a load is applied and to determine and show the actual buckling of the bridge using a camera. It comes up with a warning system to alarm engineers when a buckle occurs in the bridge. A design methodology was followed throughout the study, starting from Design Phase, Development, Testing, and Evaluation Phases. The prototype used in the study is a miniature bridge made of C-purlins, metal furring, flat bars, and bolts. The camera, esp8266 WiFi module, buzzer, and LED light are also included. During the testing of the prototype, the data gathered were a check symbol for safe truss members and while ‘ x ‘ symbol for buckled members. Results showed that the developed application could detect real-time buckling detection equipped with a warning system.

title: QUBO: A New Framework for Traffic Control in Metropolitan Cities Using Quantum Optimization Technique
authors: R. Surekha, M. Giri, T. Saharika, S. Venisree, K. Vinay, L. Vinod
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: Traffic in metropolitan cities in India was increased day by day that create a big challenge to monitor and control traffic congestion over constraint road facilities with large number of vehicles. Traditional traffic monitoring approaches are mostly depending on fixed time allotment plans or local persons execute plan as per their choice of decisions, these factors show that the traditional models fail to handle or monitor network traffic dynamically, increased heavy congestion traffic, increased journey time, and raises a very big problem to control traffic. To address all these limitations, we proposed a new framework for traffic control in metropolitan cities using quantum optimization technique (QUBO), traffic signal was automated and controlled by analyzing dynamic network traffic by considering various features, time allocation of each signal is varied to manage congestions and control traffic. The proposed approach establishes good network signal monitoring by combining methods like preprocessing of network maps, gathering and analyzing network traffic dynamically, binary encryption applied to signals, and with cooperation or coordination of network level data. Experiments are conducted to evaluate performance of proposed QUBO with other traditional methods like FTSC, ASC, AHBC, GCA, RLBC, and MPC. From the results we come to know that QUBO traffic signal control approach exhibits high accuracy of traffic congestion control, traffic is interpretable without providing over training of models, shows high robustness and high scalability, and appropriate approach to adopt by the Indian government to deploy into real time many metropolitan cities to monitor and control traffic signal signals without facing more delay time.

title: Research on Adaptive Topology Optimization for Power Communication Networks Based on an Improved Genetic Algorithm
authors: Junyi Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: To address performance degradation in power communication network topologies under high load and dynamic disturbances, an adaptive topology optimization model based on an improved genetic algorithm is proposed. Building upon traditional genetic algorithms, this model incorporates elite retention, adaptive mutation, and link status feedback mechanisms. Combined with a multi-objective normalized fitness function, it achieves dynamic topology optimization and local disturbance regulation.Experimental results demonstrate that, across 90 typical topology samples, the improved algorithm achieves average improvements of 18.7%, 12.4%, and 30.6% in path length, link utilization, and convergence stability, respectively, compared to the standard algorithm, exhibiting superior adaptability and robustness.

title: RZSL-PSR: Noise-Disentangled Retinex Zero-Shot Framework for Lunar PSR Image Enhancement
authors: Fengqi Zhang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: The low-quality optical images due to lack of illumination pose an obvious obstacle to studying the permanently shadowed regions (PSRs) at the lunar poles. However, image enhancement methods in computer vision that aim to improve image quality have problems such as incomplete assumptions, over-reliance on paired datasets, and complex operations. To obtain highly visible PSR images, this paper proposes a zero-shot learning PSR image enhancement framework (RZSL-PSR) based on Retinex decomposition theory, which reveals the obscured image content through estimated reflectance map without requiring any dataset. RZSL-PSR adds regional correlated noise map and independent noise map to the Retinex framework to facilitate the removal of structure-related noise and independent noise in the image. In addition, a feature extraction network RZSLNet is exploited to learn multiple Retinex sub-maps, in which skip connections and attention structures improve the model's capacity to output robust sub-maps. RZSL-PSR simultaneously and accurately learns the reflectance map, illumination map, regional correlated noise map, and independent noise map of the PSR image, effectively achieving image enhancement and denoising by reflectance map. Extensive experimental results on PSR images demonstrate that the reflectance map output by RZSL-PSR truly restores the internal topography of PSR and outperforms other zero-shot learning methods in multiple noreference image quality assessment metrics.

title: Vision Transformer-Based Deepfake Detection: A Comprehensive Approach for Identifying Manipulated Video Content
authors: S. Hariharasudhan, P. S. L. Risheeth, G. V. V. Satyanarayana
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: Deepfake technology has both rigor and challenges in the domain of digital authenticity as well as information security. Therefore, in this paper, a deepfake detection model will be proposed by using the Vision Transformer model in order to detect the manipulated videos. For this purpose, a ViT model has been utilized, which is “vit_large_patch16_224,” with a high accuracy of 90.01% on the Google Deep Fake Detection dataset. Advanced data augmentation methods, such as geometrical transformations as well as color transformations, have been employed in order to increase robustness against various manipulation techniques. Videos in our system are preprocessed frame by frame, while the final classification decision is obtained by majority voting. Experimental results showed superior performance with 90% precision and recall in both real and manipulated classes and an AUC of 0.954 on the ROC analysis. Also, the model has minimal overfitting with consistent convergence of training and validation accuracies, hence proving to be effective in real-world applications of deepfake detection. These findings contribute to the growing body of research into transformer-based approaches for media forensics and authentication.

title: LLM-Assisted Carbon-Aware Coordinated EV Charging for Decarbonization
authors: Chengdong Yang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: Against the backdrop of decarbonization and rapid transportation electrification, large-scale electric vehicle (EV) charging introduces both opportunities and challenges for power-system operation. On the one hand, flexible EV charging can help absorb low-carbon electricity and improve renewable-energy utilization; on the other hand, growing charging demand increases scheduling complexity under time-varying grid conditions and diverse user requirements. Conventional charging strategies are typically based on price signals, load shifting, or predefined optimization rules, but they often underuse carbon-intensity information and have limited ability to incorporate user preferences expressed in natural language. To address these issues, this paper proposes an LLM-assisted carbon-aware coordinated EV charging framework. The proposed method combines time-varying carbon intensity, electricity prices, load information, and user-side constraints within a hierarchical architecture, where a large language model (LLM) provides high-level scheduling recommendations and a lower-level optimizer minimizes carbon cost and charging smoothness under operational constraints. A case study based on real-world EV traces and CAISO carbon-intensity data compares the proposed method with four benchmark strategies: Uncontrolled, Valley Filling, Price Based, and Carbon Aware. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves the lowest average carbon emissions, reducing daily emissions from 71.55 kg in the Uncontrolled baseline to 56.13 kg, while maintaining competitive charging cost and near-complete target-SOC satisfaction. In addition, paired statistical tests confirm that the carbon-reduction improvement is significant. These results demonstrate that LLM-assisted coordinated charging provides a practical and effective approach for low-carbon EV integration.

title: LS-Interpret: A Multi-Algorithm Gradient Boosting Framework with Three-Tier SHAP Explainability for Geospatial Landslide Susceptibility Prediction
authors: Ran Huo
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Landslide susceptibility mapping (LSM) presents a challenging high-dimensional binary classification problem over heterogeneous geospatial feature spaces derived from remote sensing imagery and geographic information systems (GIS). Despite growing adoption of ensemble learning in this domain, existing machine learning-based LSM pipelines suffer from three critical limitations: (1) the absence of rigorous, unified benchmarking protocols for comparing classical and state-of-the-art gradient boosting architectures is rarely conducted under unified experimental protocols; (2) the distinct optimization mechanisms of ensemble models-including histogram-based splitting in LightGBM, ordered boosting with target-leakage prevention in CatBoost, and L2-regularized additive tree learning in XG-Boost-are insufficiently compared on high-dimensional geospatial feature spaces; and (3) model interpretability is typically restricted to single-level feature importance scores, neglecting nonlinear decision boundaries and pairwise feature interaction effects that are essential for model transparency. To address these gaps, we propose LS-Interpret, a machine learning system that integrates systematic multi-algorithm evaluation with multi-level SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) for explainable geospatial risk prediction. Three algorithmic contributions are presented: (1) a unified benchmarking pipeline evaluating nine classifiers spanning logistic regression, kernel-based SVM, tree ensembles, and gradient boosting variants on 14 remote sensing and GISderived features, with CatBoost achieving the highest test AUC of 0.80 through its ordered boosting strategy; (2) a three-tier SHAP interpretability framework encompassing global feature attribution via mean absolute Shapley values, nonlinear threshold detection through dependence plots, and pairwise interaction decomposition through Shapley interaction value matrices; and (3) a multi-configuration spatial prediction module with crossmodel spatial consistency evaluation, confirming LightGBM as the most robust spatial inference model. Experiments on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 4 4 2}$</tex> balanced samples (721 documented landslide events) validate both predictive performance and computational interpretability of the proposed system.

title: Improved Yolov8 for Monitoring of Coconut Trees Before and After the Typhoon
authors: Zheyu Han, Ning Wang, Xiaoning Yao, Tingting Wen, Xiao-Ming Li
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Typhoons frequently cause severe damage to coconut plantations in tropical coastal regions, leading to substantial economic and ecological losses. However, fine-scale quantitative assessments of such damage remain limited. In this study, a coconut tree detection method based on YOLOv8-CBAM was proposed. A UAV image dataset of coconut trees before and after typhoons was constructed, including both pre-typhoon and post-typhoon test sets. Experimental results show that the proposed YOLOv8-CBAM model achieved the best detection performance among all compared methods, with the F1-score improving by 1.7% on the pre-typhoon test set and 0.6% on the post-typhoon test set compared with the baseline YOLOv8 model. Furthermore, quantitative assessment of coconut tree damage in different regions revealed that Xinxi Village and Baishi Village experienced the highest loss rates, reaching 78.8% and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0. 7 \%}$</tex>. These findings confirm that the proposed YOLOv8-CBAM framework provides a reliable and transferable solution for rapid post-typhoon monitoring and damage quantification of tropical tree species, supporting ecological restoration and climate-resilient agricultural management.

title: From Black-Box to Explainable Clinical Support: Benchmarking ConvNeXt and Swin Transformer on the Class-Imbalanced OCTDL Dataset
authors: Azzeddine Hlali, Majid Ben Yakhlef, Soulaiman El Hazzat
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study on improving metric performance in classification of retinal diseases suggests using novel computer vision architectures for the highly imbalanced OCTDL dataset. We compare two state-of-the-art network designs, ConvNeXt (an updated CNN) and Swin Transformer (a hierarchical Vision Transformer), with the traditional ResNet50 as a reference. Our results show that ConvNeXt has the most impressive pattern recognition ability, it not only recorded the top accuracy (93.6%) but also the $\mathbf{F 1}$-score (0.887), which is a 9.0% gain over the baseline. Although the Swin Transformer considerably increased accuracy (+7.61 %), it lost in the precision parameter possibly due to the absence of inductive biases in medium-sized data regimes. Significantly, to give the ophthalmologists more trust in machine decisions, we integrated Grad-CAM explainability. This method, showing the model's predicted image areas, could potentially be a visually interpretative aid for the clinical professionals in determining whether the AI's focus is indeed on the correct pathological features.

title: Monkeypox Diagnosis Using Transfer Learning via Skin Lesion Images
authors: Ahmet Ilhan, Sedra Alialsagher Mohammed, Şerife Kaba, Boran Sekeroglu, Omid Mirzaei
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Monkeypox is a viral zoonotic disease that has recently gained global attention due to outbreaks in regions previously not endemic to it. Early detection and identification of monkeypox lesions are crucial for the effectiveness of disease management and control. This study aims to distinguish monkeypox and non-monkeypox images in the Monkeypox Skin Lesion Dataset (MSLD) using a pretrained deep learning model, MobileNetV3 S. The model achieved the highest performance with 0.96 accuracy, 0.95 sensitivity, 0.95 precision, and an F1score of 0.95 and outperformed the models in recent studies. This study demonstrated that deep learning with transfer learning can effectively detect and classify monkeypox skin lesions with high accuracy. Such automated tools have the potential to improve disease surveillance and outbreak response. The findings of this study could aid in the development of rapid and accurate diagnostic tools for monkeypox, which is crucial for controlling ongoing and future outbreaks.

title: Metaheuristic Optimization for Pavement Deformation Prediction: A Comparative Study of PSO, FA and AFSA
authors: Oumaima El Abidi, Mouna El Mkhalet, Nouzha Lamdouar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This research continues a broader study on the behavior and performance of flexible pavements under combined mechanical and thermal effects. In previous work, pavement deformation was modeled using the Boussinesq theory coupled with Monte Carlo simulations to account for variability in traffic and temperature, and predictive neural networks were developed to estimate fatigue responses and structural performance. Building on these foundations, the present study extends the analysis toward optimization by integrating three bio-inspired algorithms: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Firefly Algorithm (FA), and Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA). The optimization process aims to minimize the fatigue ratio $N / N f$ by adjusting the thicknesses and elastic moduli of the pavement layers within mechanical and geometric constraints. Four representative Moroccan cities: Casablanca, Marrakech, Fès, and Meknès were selected to reflect diverse climatic and traffic conditions. All algorithms produced feasible and realistic designs, while the Firefly Algorithm demonstrated the best balance between convergence speed, computational efficiency, and result stability. Overall, this work reinforces the continuity of a comprehensive research framework that combines physical modeling, data-driven prediction, and intelligent optimization. The approach contributes to advancing pavement engineering studies in Morocco by promoting the use of hybrid numerical and artificial-intelligence methods for more reliable and adaptive road design.

title: RHLMA: A Role-Hierarchical LLM Multi-Agent Framework for Airborne Software Requirement Review
authors: Jinghua Sun, Rongtai Yang, Jingjing Gu, Lili Chen, Yige Zhao, Xin Yang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multi-scale Artificial Intelligence (MAI)
abstract: The development of airborne software must strictly adhere to airworthiness standards such as DO-178C, where a rigorous review of requirements is crucial to ensure software quality and flight safety. However, existing automated methods encounter three critical limitations when processing the complex semantics of airborne software requirements: poor domain knowledge adaptation, inefficient multi-agent collaboration, and trustworthiness degradation caused by LLM overconfidence. To address these issues, this paper proposes RHLMA, a review framework based on Role-Hierarchical Large Language Model Multi-Agent collaboration. First, we construct a multi-task instruction dataset for the airborne domain and enhance the base model’s domain adaptation capability via supervised fine-tuning. Subsequently, a hierarchical collaborative architecture is designed, comprising task coordination, specialized review, and trusted aggregation. Through role-specific division of labor and hierarchical communication, the framework significantly reduces interaction overhead while ensuring comprehensive, multi-dimensional review depth. Furthermore, we integrate a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module and a confidence calibration mechanism to dynamically reference authoritative knowledge, thereby improving the trustworthiness and traceability of the review outcomes. Experiments conducted on real-world airborne software requirement data demonstrate that RHLMA substantially outperforms baseline methods on core review metrics, achieving higher accuracy alongside efficiency and reliability. The proposed framework thus offers an efficient and trustworthy solution for automated requirement review in safety-critical domains.

title: Analysis of Digital Twin Ecosystem for the Renal System: Improving Patient Outcomes in Chronic Renal Disease and Dialysis Need Prediction
authors: A. Jaya Mabel Rani, Raed Alazaidah, V. Samuthira Pandi, Balpreet Singh, J. Alekhya, R. Ruth Shobitha
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Chronic Renal Disease (CRD) is a global healthcare issue due to its progression, high treatment costs, and impact on patient quality of life. Early disease progression detection and precise dialysis prediction are crucial yet difficult for clinicians. Traditional clinical assessment methods use periodic laboratory testing and static predictive models, which often miss renal system physiological changes. A Digital Twin ecosystem for the renal system is investigated as a novel way to improve patient outcomes and dialysis requirement prediction. The term "renal digital twin" refers to a computer model of a patient's kidneys that uses real-time simulations of disease progression based on clinical data, physiological factors, and medical history. Early intervention, therapy optimization, and risk stratification are all made possible by the digital twin framework's tailored modeling and predictive analytics. The suggested ecosystem tracks renal function, looks for patterns of decline, and estimates when a patient will need dialysis by integrating real-time data collection, computer modeling, and smart analytics. When compared to more traditional methods, experimental assessments show that analysis based on digital twins provides better prediction accuracy and decision support. This study demonstrates how digital twin technology can revolutionize nephrology by enhancing patient-centered treatment, reducing healthcare burden, and facilitating proactive disease management in the context of chronic renal disease.

title: EEG-based Machine and Deep Learning Approaches for Alzheimer’s and Frontotemporal Dementia: A Systematic Review of the OpenNeuro ds004504 Dataset
authors: Mahdoubi Youness, Zrira Nabila, Aaibid Hind, Elafi Issam, Ngote Nabil, Benmiloud Ibtissam
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: One of the main challenges in clinical diagnosis is the early and accurate differentiation between Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and frontotemporal dementia (FTD) due to the resemblance of many clinical symptoms and the presence of varied EEG patterns. This makes electroencephalography (EEG) appealing, as it is affordable, non-invasive and provides superior time measurement capabilities for identifying preliminary brain function abnormalities. However, the EEG-based studies present a major challenge for comparative evaluation because of the large differences in their preprocessing steps, feature extraction choices, and validation methods. In this paper, we focus specifically on studies that used the same EEG dataset: OpenNeuro ds004504, which includes standardized resting-state EEG data from patients with AD, FTD, and healthy control participants. We identified and reviewed ten studies following PRISMA guidelines across four primary research areas: preprocessing pipelines, EEG representations, machine learning and deep learning models, and validation strategies. Our findings show recurring patterns, including theta and alpha activity changes and brain connectivity disruptions. We also highlight several methodological limitations, such as dataset imbalance, inconsistent evaluation approaches, and limited model interpretability. Comparing performance metrics across studies revealed clear differences between machine learning and deep learning approaches, showing that validation strategy has a major impact on study outcomes. Clinically, EEG-based models show potential to provide early dementia screening solutions; however, these models still need to be more robust and interpretable before they can realistically be used in clinical practice. Overall, this review gives a structured comparison of EEG-based methods for dementia classification and suggests practical directions for future EEG research on AD and FTD through better reproducibility, reliability and clinical applicability.

title: Multi-Path Parallel Quantum Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Han Qi, Xintong Huo, Abdullah Gani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multi-scale Artificial Intelligence (MAI)
abstract: Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks (QCNN) migrate the convolution and pooling concepts of classical Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to variational quantum circuits, showing potential on Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. However, traditional QCNN adopts a single-depth circuit structure, which has limitations in multi-scale feature extraction, and is prone to the barren plateau phenomenon during training, making it difficult to adapt to high-dimensional real-world scenarios such as network intrusion detection. This paper proposes a QCNN architecture based on a multi-path parallel structure. For the network intrusion detection task, it draws on the Inception module to construct three parallel quantum convolution paths: shallow, medium, and deep, achieving hierarchical extraction of multi-scale features; residual connections are introduced to preserve encoded state information and provide gradient shortcuts; a hybrid optimization strategy combining classical Adam and Quantum Natural Gradient (QNG) is adopted to adapt to the heterogeneous parameter space. Experimental validation shows that, on the UNSW-NB15 network intrusion detection dataset, the proposed Multi-Path Parallel Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (MP-QCNN) improves detection accuracy by 3% compared to traditional single-path QCNN and effectively mitigates the barren plateau phenomenon. This design strictly maintains NISQ depth constraints and quantum parallelism advantages, providing an efficient and stable feature extraction framework for high-dimensional tasks such as network intrusion detection.

title: Design of an Intelligent Information System for Spaceborne Remote Sensing Data Processing
authors: Tong Liu
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: The massive volume and diverse formats of spaceborne remote sensing data pose challenges for intelligent processing. This paper designs an intelligent information processing system tailored for spaceborne remote sensing data. It adopts a microservices architecture to construct the system framework, employs deep learning for multi-source data fusion and intelligent target recognition, and utilizes a distributed realtime processing engine to address large-scale data processing issues. Field validation in disaster prevention and mitigation, urban planning, and agricultural census applications demonstrates that this solution significantly enhances remote sensing data processing efficiency and recognition accuracy, offering a novel pathway for intelligent applications of space-based remote sensing data.

title: Preliminary assessment of how land cover changes can affect temperature in urban areas
authors: Davide Morale, Emanuele Alcaras, Paola Mercogliano
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Urbanization is one of the most significant drivers of environmental change, altering land cover patterns and modifying surface energy balances. These transformations have profound implications for urban microclimates, particularly in terms of Land Surface Temperature (LST), which is strongly influenced by the type of land cover and their extent. Understanding how specific land cover transitions contribute to surface warming is crucial for mitigating the Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect and supporting sustainable urban planning. This study investigates the impact of land cover changes on Land Surface Temperature (LST) in Rome between 2000, 2015 and 2025 using Landsat satellite imagery. Land cover classification is performed using the Random Forest algorithm, while LST is derived using the Single Channel Method. The Difference in Differences (DiD) approach is then adopted to determine temperature variations as a function of the type of land cover change, isolating any climatological factors. The results indicate an average increase in $\mathbf{L S T}$ of $\mathbf{1. 1}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$ in pixels converted to builtup areas between 2000 and 2025, with more marked increases when converting tree crops to impervious surfaces $\left(6^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$, while conversion of bare soil resulted in marginal differences. These results confirm the fundamental role of vegetation in mitigating temperatures, providing valuable information for sustainable urban planning strategies.

title: Design of a retributive optimal cooperative energy trading and its blockchain-based implementation
authors: Ahmed Khoumsi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Smart grids (more briefly, grids) are responsible for producing electricity and supplying it to customers in their area. To achieve this objective while securely and efficiently adapting to changing customer needs, grids are interconnected so that they can cooperate by exchanging electricity when needed. We first develop optimal cooperative energy trading (OCET), an efficient way for exchanging electricity between grids to satisfy customer needs while minimizing the total cost of electricity production and transmission and without exceeding production and transmission capacities. We also design a retributive extension of OCET (ROCET) by handling faulty grids. To this end, we develop a method to detect faulty grids and compute corresponding penalties. A reliable smart contract in blockchain is designed to ensure that electricity production and transmission as well as payments (for electricity and penalties) conform to ROCET.

title: Real-Time Tea Leaf Disease Detection Using Attention-Driven YOLO Framework for Precision Plantation Management
authors: Punit Kumar, Vishnu Sharma, Dinesh Goyal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: Tea had always been a big part of daily life in South Asia and areas such as India relied on tea plantations extremely heavily for economic and agricultural growth. Early diagnosis of tea leaf diseases had been key to maintaining a healthy plantation, and helping to improve production, but majority of plantations relied on manual techniques to spot disease. That traditional process had been slow, inconsistent while failing to support large estates in real time. Due to such a gap, this study aims at developing a real-time tea leaf disease detection system of which is based on cutting-edge object detection techniques and attention mechanism to address accuracy, speed, and usability in real-world scenarios. The proposed system adopted a modified YOLO system in which the attention blocks were combined to the backbone of the system to highlight disease-specific textural patterns and eliminate noisy backgrounds from natural fields. The framework had been trained and validated against a custom dataset of tea leaves from the plantation zones across India with around 6,000 images taken from different light and angle conditions as well as occlusion effects. Different approaches such as data augmentation, re-calibrating the anchor boxes and transfer learning have been used to achieve a better generalization. During the evaluation, the attention powered YOLO model reported mean Average Precision of 98.9%, precision of 97.7% and recall of 95.2% with an inference speed of 34 FPS on a mid-range GPU, which proved it to be suitable for real-time applications.

title: Random Forest based Predictive Vehicle Breakdown Management System for Intelligent Roadside Assistance
authors: G.Mariammal, Gnana Moorthi P, Jona Jebarson J, Dhayaa Nidhi A, Arulkumaar K
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Unexpected vehicle breakdowns remain a significant concern in modern transportation environments due to increasing vehicle density, complex mechanical systems, and unpredictable operational conditions. Roadside failures often result in traffic congestion, safety risks, delayed emergency response, and financial losses associated with repair and towing services. Conventional vehicle maintenance strategies rely on periodic servicing schedules and manual inspection practices that fail to reflect real-time operational conditions of vehicle components. Such reactive approaches frequently allow hidden mechanical issues to progress until sudden failure occurs during travel. The proposed work introduces an Intelligent Real-Time Roadside Vehicle Assistance and Predictive Breakdown Management System designed to identify potential vehicle failure risks before critical breakdown events occur. The system integrates vehicle operational parameters, historical maintenance information, and performance indicators within a unified predictive framework. Collected vehicle data undergo systematic preprocessing to remove inconsistencies and to prepare structured feature sets representing mechanical health conditions. A machine learning based predictive model analyzes operational attributes including engine temperature, engine speed, mileage patterns, oil pressure levels, battery health status, fuel consumption behavior, and vehicle service history. Prediction outputs are converted into a dynamic risk score that reflects the likelihood of mechanical failure. The framework also provides a web-based monitoring interface for real-time vehicle health assessment, historical risk analysis, and preventive maintenance guidance. Automated alert mechanisms activate when risk levels exceed safety thresholds, allowing early intervention before roadside failure occurs. The system additionally supports route safety evaluation and maintenance recommendations to enhance travel reliability and reduce unexpected disruptions. Overall evaluation demonstrates high predictive accuracy and reliable detection capability for potential vehicle failures. The proposed system contributes to improved transportation safety, reduced maintenance costs, and proactive vehicle health management through data-driven predictive analytics.

title: Bridging Knowledge and Reasoning: The Role of Large Language Models in AI Evolution
authors: Manish Gupta, Dibyahash Bordoloi, Peraschi Selvan Subramanian, Poornima Ediga, Mina Kumari, Sandhya N
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Large Language Models, which unify large knowledge representation with sophisticated reasoning capability, would continue to drive the rapid development of artificial intelligence. These models go beyond pattern recognition and combine contextual knowledge, factual basis and adaptability to problems. The new paradigm puts language as the connecting point of both humans-like thinking and machine learning and allows systems to acquire intent and form relationships and come up with consistent insights, cross-domain. Greater architectural sophistication, multi- modal learning and learning with auditory devices enhance a stronger alignment between learning and logical inference. With the ongoing increase in the goals of LLMs, they affect the scientific discovery, decision support, and automated content creation and provoke the critical debate on transparency, reliability, and ethical deployment. Their discussion on the knowledge structuring and reasoning provides a more thorough insight into what AI intelligence will be in the future as machines will be able to examine information, defend their output, and help people understand by being responsible. This trend is a revolutionary development of AI that can significantly work together in complex cognitive activities.

title: Bridge Structural Health Monitoring Using Vision Transformers and Graph Neural Networks
authors: Qasim Aljamal, Mahmoud Aljamal, Mohammad Aljaidi, Ayoub Alsarhan, Maruf Farhan, Wael Hadi, Hayel Khafajeh, Ahmad Abdullah Alshammari
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Bridges are important in the transportation infrastructure, yet in the absence of efficient monitoring, they may deteriorate and fail structurally. Some of the most intricate patterns of damage can be very difficult to detect by traditional structural health monitoring (SHM) systems because they rely on manual features and threshold-based analysis. These methods are weak in their adapting to the different conditions of the environment, aging, and nonlinear structural behavior that can result in a late detection of damages, and wrong evaluation of the conditions. These weaknesses bring to the fore the need to have more intelligent and data-driven forms of monitoring that can record the subtle and dynamic structural anomalies in real time. To enhance SHM, a new model combining Vision Transformers (ViTs) and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is proposed to identify and identify anomalies. The vibration and strain are converted to image-like data of ViTs, which learns long-range correlation. GNN subsequently determines the structural similarities of space. The highest accuracy of this ViT-GNN network was 99.2 percent, and it exceeded the traditional one, which implied its applicability in intelligent and real-time bridge monitoring.

title: Finite Element Analysis and Design Optimization of an Electric Vehicle Chassis
authors: Moussa Kalli Salissou, Yassine Zahidi, Mohamed El Moufid, Wafaa Dachry, Hassan Gziri, Hicham Medromi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: The optimized design of a vehicle chassis plays a key role in ensuring mechanical performance, weight efficiency, and safety in electric vehicles, where mass directly affects energy consumption. This study presents a structured workflow combining computer-aided design (CAD), finite element analysis (FEA), and topology optimization (TO) to develop a lightweight and mechanically efficient chassis structure. The initial chassis model was designed based on realistic dimensional constraints of a typical urban electric vehicle, including battery and suspension mounting zones. Static load cases representative of real operating conditions were applied, particularly on the floor and front regions of the chassis. The structural behavior was evaluated using equivalent elastic deformation and von Mises stress criteria. Analysis of the initial design revealed localized stress concentrations and regions with limited structural contribution, indicating potential for mass reduction. Based on these findings, a density-based topology optimization was carried out in ANSYS Workbench with the objective of minimizing mass while maintaining structural integrity. The optimization process converged after approximately 28 iterations, targeting a material reduction of $70 \%$. Following the optimization results, a new chassis configuration was reconstructed while considering manufacturing constraints. The redesigned structure was then re-evaluated under the same loading conditions. The results showed a significant improvement, achieving a mass reduction exceeding 30% while keeping stresses below the material’s elastic limit. In addition, the fatigue analysis, conducted to assess the durability of the chassis, demonstrated a long fatigue life and a sufficient safety margin. These findings highlight the effectiveness of topology optimization as a practical and reliable tool for designing lightweight and structurally sound vehicle components.

title: Context-Aware Pervasive Computing Framework for Intelligent Smart Space Environments
authors: Lalit Sachdeva, Priya Vij
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The rapid growth of pervasive computing, Internet of Things (IoT), and artificial intelligence has enabled the development of intelligent smart spaces capable of sensing, reasoning, and responding to dynamic environments. However, many existing systems face challenges in integrating heterogeneous contextual data, performing adaptive decision-making, and maintaining real-time responsiveness. The proposal in this study is a context aware pervasive computing architecture that is aimed at supporting intelligent behavior in smart space. The structure incorporates the multi-source context acquisition, semantic context modeling, hybrid reasoning and adaptive decision-making using learning capabilities. This framework was tested in a simulated smart space environment that has included heterogeneous sensors, wearable devices, smart appliances, and user interaction cases. The performance of the systems in the experiment shows that it performs well, with context recognition accuracy of 94.2, decision accuracy of 91.5, predictive context accuracy of 89.8 and an average response latency of 112 ms. The framework also increased energy usage by 18.7 percent with the system still scoring high user satisfaction (8.7/10) with an action throughput of 24 actions one second. These results indicate the efficacy of semantic modelling and adaptive intelligence on enhancing responsiveness, personalization and cost-effectiveness of pervasive smart environments.

title: From Static Surveys to Adaptive Questionnaires: A Systematic Review of Generative AI–Driven Personalization in Education
authors: Saif AbuAisheh, Ying Wah Teh, Liyana Shuib
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Personalized assessment is a cornerstone of adaptive learning and learner analytics, yet traditional static surveys and questionnaires often fail to reflect learners’ unique needs or evolving understanding. Recent progress in generative artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large language models (LLMs) and natural-language processing systems, enables questionnaires to be generated, adapted, and scored dynamically. This systematic literature review synthesizes peer-reviewed research published between 2019 and 2025 on generative-AI-driven personalization in educational questionnaires. Following PRISMA-inspired procedures, we defined research questions, searched four digital libraries (IEEE Xplore, Scopus, Web of Science, and SpringerLink). The synthesis highlights how generative models support scalable item production, personalized question sequencing, and adaptive feedback across cognitive levels. At the same time, the literature reports recurring limitations including hallucinated or irrelevant items, bias and fairness risks, privacy concerns, and continued reliance on human supervision. Evidence on long-term learning outcomes and governance remains limited. We consolidate current applications, summarize benefits and challenges, and identify research gaps and future directions, including hybrid approaches that integrate structured knowledge (e.g., knowledge graphs) with generation, transparent and fair item-generation pipelines, and cross-disciplinary evaluation of adaptive questionnaires.

title: Unveiling the Drivers of E-Grocery Usage Behaviour in Urban Areas
authors: Yuka Maheswara, Yulia Magdalena, Yuli Eni
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: E-grocery has recently expanded in Indonesia because of Covid-19. However, the adoption rate of E-grocery is considerably lower than other e-commerce platforms. In order to investigate this, this study used UTAUT to find what influences the adoption rate of the consumers towards $\mathbf{E}$ Grocery. 103 respondents completed a questionnaire. The results show that the main factors influencing the rate of e-grocery adoption are Social Influence and Facilitating Conditions. While Facilitating Conditions have a significant impact on Use Behaviour, Social Influence has a significant impact on Behavioural Intention. It was discovered that neither Performance Expectancy nor Effort Expectancy significantly affects Behavioural Intention. These findings show that social factors and technological support have a greater influence on e-grocery adoption in Indonesian cities than perceived effectiveness and ease of use.

title: Emergence of artificial intelligence in electroencephalogram analysis
authors: Salma El Kake, Fatima Ezahra Mouas, Soukaina Ismouguene, Hanae Terchoune, Taoufiq Fechtali
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a noninvasive method used in cognitive neuroscience and clinical neurology for the assessment of cerebral activity. Despite significant technological advancements in EEG, it has always depended on manual examination and conventional signal processing, both of which are complexes and laborious. The advent of artificial intelligence (AI) has significantly influenced EEG analysis by allowing automated features extraction and complex modeling of sophisticated brain signals. Machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) approaches now augment EEG-based diagnostics, cognitive evaluations and clinical neurology applications.

title: Lightweight Machine Learning for Intrusion Detection in Zero-Trust Systems
authors: Yasmein Abaza, Hana' Alrashid
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Zero Trust security architectures have gained attention as an effective approach for mitigating modern cyber threats; however, their practical deployment in intrusion detection systems often relies on computationally intensive deep learning models, which limit their applicability in real-time and resource-constrained environments. This study evaluates whether lightweight machine learning models can provide effective intrusion detection under Zero Trust continuous verification principles. A quantitative experimental approach is adopted using lightweight classifiers, including XGBoost, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, and Gaussian Naïve Bayes, evaluated on the CICIDS2017 intrusion detection dataset. The results demonstrate that ensemble-based lightweight models achieve high detection performance, with XGBoost outperforming other models and reaching an accuracy of 99.88% and an F1-score of 99.89%, while simpler probabilistic models exhibit comparatively weaker performance. In addition to detection accuracy, computational efficiency is assessed using training time and inference time, showing that XGBoost and Random Forest maintain competitive execution times suitable for real-time operation. These findings indicate that high intrusion detection performance can be achieved without relying on complex deep learning architectures and highlight the potential of lightweight machine learning models as practical alternatives for Zero Trust intrusion detection, particularly in resource-constrained environments, and support their suitability for real-time continuous verification.

title: Multi-Modal Perception for Intelligent Robotic Systems
authors: Amandeep Nagpal, Sanjeev Kukreti, Pawan Kumar, Sanjoy Das, A. Samatha, Nitika Vats Doohan
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Complex evaluations required of the intelligent robots necessitate a solid and contextualized view that cut across limited frameworks of sensory observation. Classical methods have the attributes of not being able to adapt to ambiguity, sensor noise, and no full environmental versatility prohibiting resourcefulness of the system to dynamical real-world circumstances. The paper is a solution to these challenges by introducing AURA-Net (Adaptive Unified Representation and Attention Network) a new framework that ought to be employed in achieving a robust multi-modal perception. The suggested system combines sensor streams into a single intelligent output format in terms of modality-specialized encoders. AURA-Net is based on a rather different paradigm of adaptive attention, though it uses hierarchies instead of conventional early/late fusion, such that modality relative weights are shifted dynamically depending on the environmental conditions. This allows the system to give priority to the most trustful cues, e.g., in noisy environments providing emphasis on tactile sense, or in cluttered ones provide the focus on visual. Furthermore, a hybrid neuro-symbolic reasoning module is added to combine the learned representations with the domain knowledge that will make it more explainable and capable of decision making under uncertainty. More perception and control policy refining is effected by feedback driven by reinforcement in an attempt to achieve the long-term adaptability. The experimental evaluations in all the tasks of navigation, manipulation, and human-robot interaction show that AURA-Net is much more robust, more precise, and tougher to execute specific tasks than the benchmarks of state-of-the-art technologies. The paper sets the stage towards the design of intelligent robots with resilient and human-like perception. The proposed AURA-Net approach attains a general accuracy of 98.8 percent over a variety of robotic perception and decision-making tasks.

title: Offline Photo-Shield Automatic System for Finding and Redacting Privacy-Sensitive Information in Images
authors: Sandhya B Shettennavar, Shefali Sharma, Aswathy J S, Kaluvayi Sai Rakshitha, Karthika R
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The Photo-shield Automatic System presents a fully offline, AI-driven image redaction system for protecting sensitive information in digital images. This system automatically detects faces and text using EasyOCR and Media Pipe, respectively. The extracted text is analyzed using Python-based classifiers and REGEX techniques to identify privacy-critical entities. It recognizes Aadhaar, PAN numbers, phone numbers, email IDs, addresses, date of birth, financial details and vehicle registration plates. The detected faces and sensitive text regions are classified into predefined categories. Appropriate masking techniques, such as Gaussian blur or blackout are applied using OpenCV. This entire pipeline operates offline, ensuring data security and eliminating cloud dependency. A predefined risk-scoring module is used to evaluate the severity of privacy for each image. An automated PDF report of risk analysis summarizes the detected and redacted regions. The experimental results show high accuracy across multiple images and environment contexts.

title: Rethinking AI Ethics in Education: From Restrictive Policies to Learning-Oriented Design
authors: Suhailah Mohamed Noor, Zaid Momani
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: The rapid adoption of generative artificial intelligence in education has prompted many institutions to adopt restrictive policies framing AI ethics as compliance and control. While aiming to protect assessment integrity, such approaches risk limiting learning opportunities and fail to cultivate students’ ethical judgment. This conceptual paper argues that AI ethics in education should be reconceptualized as a pedagogical issue rather than purely regulatory. Drawing on learning design and process-oriented assessment principles, we develop Prompt Chaining with Human-in-the-Loop as a structured pedagogical methodology for cultivating ethical AI use. Prompt Chaining is conceptualized as a cognitive practice promoting iterative questioning, critical evaluation, and intellectual responsibility through structured, multi-step interactions. Human-in-the-Loop functions as the integrated oversight mechanism ensuring human judgment and accountability remain central throughout the learning process. By embedding ethical reasoning into everyday learning activities, this approach shifts discourse from restriction to learning and from compliance to responsibility, offering educators a pedagogically grounded pathway for fostering ethical AI use aligned with critical thinking, metacognition, and lifelong learning.

title: Secure Edge–Fog Architecture for Social IoT and Opportunistic Network Applications
authors: Nidhi Mishra, Utkarsh Anand
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: While existing research often surveys edge-fog computing in static or infrastructure-dependent contexts, this study addresses a critical gap by designing and testing a novel, unified secure edge-fog architecture specifically tailored for Social Internet of Things (SIoT) and opportunistic network services. Working in resource-limited and intermittently connected environments, the proposed framework is unique in that it combines social awareness with decentralized security to reduce the use of centralized cloud infrastructure. It designed a multi-layer architecture, which involved socially related IoT devices, local processing nodes (edge nodes), and regional coordination nodes (fog nodes). Its most important contribution is a model of composite trust evaluation and a dynamic social graph that, when combined, make it possible to communicate safely via store-carry-forward capabilities, without any prior trust. In order to have the highest performance, the architecture will enable adaptive computation offloading and privacy-preserving preprocessing of data. The simulation outcomes with different node movement and attack conditions show that there are great improvements compared to cloud-centric architectures and non-social ones. Namely, the proposed system reported an average communication latency of 72 ms (as compared to 185 ms in cloud-centric models) and minimized the energy usage to 53 J as compared to 92 J. Moreover, the packet delivery ratio rose with 94% out of 78% respectively in comparison with cloud-centric systems, whereas the decentralized trust system made it very stable and rapid convergence to isolate malicious nodes. These results are a confirmation that social consciousness and decentralized trust embedded within an edge-fog architecture are an effective solution offering resilience and high performance in real-world use in dynamically changing, infrastructure-constrained SIoT environments.

title: Enhancing EEG Data Quality with Explainable AI-Based Artifact Detection Models
authors: Shweta Bansal, Rajesh Kumar Verma, Amit Dutt, Rohan Verma, Apoorva Busad, R. P. Ram Kumar
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: The electroencephalography (EEG) is still an important objective of measuring neural activity, but due to eye movements, a contraction of the absorption, cardiac consecutive rhythm, and interference with the environment, the credibility of its interpretation is generally inferior. The classic practices of residential filtering experiences the risk of suppressed fundamental cues of the neural system whereas contemporary machine learning technologies often cannot be interpreted and adjusted to heterogeneous databases. To solve these issues, this article proposes, (Neurophysiological Clean and Explainable Network) Neuro CleanX-Net, a sophisticated explainable network that applies to efficient artifact detection and the improvements of the EEG signal. The proposed algorithm combines multi-resolution concentration of the wavelet-convolutional coding with the fusion of cross-domain attention on the video to allocate discriminative time-space features and stress on artifact-prone regions. Self-monitored contrastive learning method also allows the model to learn well with few labeled training data, and adaptive graph neural filter optimizes the inter-channel dependencies to form intricate propagation detail of artifacts amongst electrode networks. Moreover, interpretability will be guaranteed with the usage of Shapley-based attribution mapping that will give clear localization of the influence of artifacts on the frequency range and electrode regions. This bilateral nature of artifact classification and clean signal reconstruction increases the value of EEG in downstream applications, such as brain computer interfaces, seizure disclosed and monitored cognitive state classification. NeuroCleanX-Net levers clinical understanding about the development of an exemplar scalable and interpretable basis of the EEG data quality of technique in the research and healthcare realm. The NeuroCleanX-Net demonstrated an overall accuracy of 96.6% in artifact identification and signal augmentation.

title: Autonomous Multi-Agent Cyber Defense for Mitigating AI-Generated Threats
authors: Prajwalasimha S N, Dilip Kumar Jang Bahadur Saini, Ananya S, Abhiram B
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The fast development of AI-based cyber threats has essentially changed the situation regarding the attacker defender. This brings in adaptive, automated and polymorphic attacks behaviors that impact on the old centralized and rule-based security systems. The given paper introduces an independent multi-agent cyber defense system that represents cyber protection as a decentralized and collaborative decision-making system in adversarial uncertainty. In the suggested architecture, every defense entity acts as the intelligent agent with the localized perception, autonomous reasoning, and adaptive learning abilities, and the resilience of the system level becomes a result of the well-organized inter-agent coordination and sharing information. Theoretically, the framework models the critical properties needed to address countering intelligent opponents, such as adaptability to non-stationary threat, scalability to large networked systems, and resilience to partial observability and component failures. In order to prove the proposed formulation, comprehensive experiments with representative Artificial Intelligence (AI)-generated attack cases are used. The findings indicate that instead of centralized and single-agent security solutions, the multi-agent approach has higher detection accuracy and low response latency, and it is more robust. In addition, the experimental discussion proves the scalability and deployment ability of the framework to a wide range of network and threat levels. The paper is also a single and expandable reference framework of next-generation autonomous cyber defense with both theoretical and empirical values to justify the implementation of multi-agent intelligence in contemporary and future cyber infrastructures.

title: Hybrid Wind-Solar Grid Integrated Energy System Simulation with MPPT Control In Matlab/Simulink
authors: Indrajeet Kumar, Sonali R.Nandanwar, Saket Bihari, Anand Singh, Sandeep Kumar Wasnik, Sonu Ahirwar
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Many countries are switching to renewable energy sources including wind, tidal, and photovoltaic (PV) systems as electricity demand increases and fossil fuel supplies decline. Particularly, PV systems have generated a lot of interest as a means of producing electricity. Integrating PV systems with grid-connected multilevel inverters is essential to get the most of them. In this study, a multilevel inverter (MLI) depending on an area-interconnected T-type inverter topology is used to mimic a grid-connected photovoltaic system. PV sources are coupled in series to increase input voltage in order to accomplish efficient power transmission, which creates difficulties for traditional inverter circuits. The proposed inverter operates in a three-level buck mode and delivers a maximum output of +Vdc/2. This design combines the advantages of conventional two-level inverters, including lower conduction losses and easier operation, with advantages like lower total harmonic distortion (THD), fewer filter size, and lower switching losses. It also ensures reduced voltage stress and peak inverse voltage across switches. Additionally, a hybrid pulse width modulation (PWM) strategy combining level-shifted and phase-shifted techniques is used to simplify gate pulse generation. Simulation results in MATLAB confirm the system’s performance and control effectiveness.

title: Variational Enhancement of Coded Fringes via Bi-directional Coded Content Similarity for Structured-light 3D Measurement
authors: Wei Cao, Xiaodong Na, Tianzhe Bao, Zhan Song, Shengquan Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In structured-light (SL) 3D measurement systems, underexposed coded fringe images (UCFIs) often fail to decode and reconstruct sparse point clouds due to low visibility. Unfortunately, existing low-light enhancement algorithms primarily target natural non-coded images, cannot faithfully preserve the encoded information of the fringe patterns, and are prone to introducing undesirable visual artifacts, noise, and reconstruction distortion. To this end, we first propose a novel bidirectional coded content similarity (B2CS) rule, specifically designed to enhance UCFIs captured by SL systems. Based on this rule, it reveals that the failure reason of existing methods occurs by breaking coded content similarity between under- and well-exposed fringes, and accordingly we construct a hybrid L2-L1-L0 norm-constrained decomposition model with illumination-reflectance-noise priors. Considering the non-convexity caused by the joint priors, we design an effective variational decomposition framework and employ the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) for efficient optimization. Based on the reliable decomposed solution, the enhanced UCFIs can assist the structured light algorithm in recovering structurally rich and high-density point clouds. Extensive experiments on real-world SL datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both subjective and objective evaluation, and significantly improves the completeness of the 3D reconstructed point cloud (by ≥ 20 %).

title: ACT-Net: Adaptive Compensation Transformer Network for Real-Time Detection in Remote Sensing Multimodal Imagery
authors: Hengyu Guo, Shiyu Yang, Qunyong Wu, Zhanchao Huang, Mengmeng Li, Yuhang Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Real-time RGB-Thermal (RGB-T) object detection using embedded remote sensing devices, such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), has become a research hotspot in the fields of remote sensing image intelligent processing and computer vision. However, the imaging characteristics of multimodal data present the core challenge of how to effectively fuse multimodal information while maintaining a lightweight model, without overshadowing the advantages of individual modalities. To address these issues, we propose an efficient embedded detection network, ACT-Net. Firstly, a pixel-level fusion strategy, Embedded Pixel-level Multimodal Fusion (EPMF), is utilized to accommodate the multimodal information fusion requirements of various embedded devices. Then, we introduce a Visual Feature Extraction Block (VFE-Block) that explicitly aggregates local, surrounding, and global context. This design increases the effective receptive field while preserving edges at low computational cost, enabling robust feature extraction for multimodal inputs. Furthermore, we propose an Adaptive Compensation Module (ACM) based on pixel-level fusion, which combines the lightweight advantage of pixel-level fusion with the precision performance of feature-level fusion. Lastly, we introduce the Partition Spatial Attention (PSA), which effectively addresses the problem of edge information loss during feature processing. Experimental results on the DroneVehicle and M3FD multimodal datasets show that, compared to RT-DETR-r18, ACT-Net achieves improvements of 2.0% and 3.8% in mAP, respectively. Furthermore, the model reduces the number of parameters by 38.4%, and achieves a detection speed of 66 FPS on the Jetson AGX Xavier under TensorRT-accelerated FP32 end-to-end deployment, thus meeting the real-time requirements of RGB-T object detection. The source codes are available at: https://github.com/AlanGwok/ACT-Net

title: Distributed Fixed-Time Control With Adaptive Barrier Function in AC Microgrid Under Dynamic Event-Triggered Mechanism
authors: Chanjuan Zhao, Yue Li, Wei Sun, Qingwei Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a novel dynamic event-triggered (DET) fixed-time control method to solve the practical voltage and frequency consensus problem in the islanded microgrid. By introducing a new dynamic variable with multi-scale negative self-feedback terms rather than a constant, a more serious and time-variant triggering condition is proposed to reduce triggering instants and controller updates. Additionally, an adaptive barrier function and a disturbance observation-like method are integrated into the fixed-time control to enhance precise convergence control. Furthermore, the microgrid fixed-time consensus analysis and Zeno behavior exclusion are verified by the Lyapunov stability theorem. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of our proposed control.

title: IEEE Foundation
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine

title: Hierarchical Multi-Terminal DC System for Large-Scale Renewable Integration: Topology and Coordinated Control
authors: Tian Hou, Ying Huang, Guoteng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: As DC power projects transition toward phased construction and higher voltage levels, interconnecting existing systems to form a multi-voltage-level DC grid is a pragmatic choice that balances the wide-area integration of large-scale renewable energy with the maximization of legacy asset utilization. While DC/DC transformer (DCT) topologies provide viable technical paths, scaling them to ultra-high-voltage applications involves considerations such as full-scale hardware requirements and isolation transformer design. To facilitate more flexible phased expansion, this paper proposes a Hierarchical Multi-Terminal DC (HMTDC) system with Coordinated Primary and Secondary Control (CPSC). The topology employs Multi-Voltage-Level Interconnecting MMCs (MVLI-MMCs) rated for the inter-system voltage differential rather than full terminal voltage, enabling gigawatt-level power exchange through significantly reduced converter capacity. The CPSC combines distributed droop-based primary control for rapid disturbance rejection with secondary optimization that coordinates power margins and voltage quality across subsystems. Comprehensive time-domain simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework not only optimizes cross-level power flows but also ensures fault ride-through and rapid recovery under severe AC/DC contingencies, thereby providing a techno-economically viable pathway for evolving multi-voltage-level DC grids.

title: Passivity-Based Pinning Synchronization Control of Switched Directed Networks With Improved Switching Strategy
authors: Chongyi Cui, Hong Sang, Yi Liu, Peng Wang, Shuanghe Yu, Georgi Marko Dimirovski
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This research delves into the passivity-based pinning synchronization problem for switched directed networks (SDNs) within the context of a sampled-data-based event-triggered (SDET) communication environment. An improved state-dependent switching strategy (SDSS) using sampled measurements alongside a novel time-dependent Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional (LKF) approach is constructed. Subsequently, a relaxed passivity analysis framework is then presented, ensuring that the resultant synchronization error system (SES) remains passive, and that the examined SDNs achieve asymptotic synchronization in the absence of external inputs. Unlike conventional strategies, the proposed switching protocol depends exclusively on system states at discrete sampling instants and effectively utilizes historical state data, yielding a less conservative theoretical framework for passivity synthesis in SDNs. Furthermore, the resulting synchronization criterion is independent of the number of network nodes, rendering it suitable for SDNs with large size.
