Ultimately, two examples involving relevant application in image encryption and decryption are provided to verify the effectiveness and applicability of the proposed scheme, and highlight the distinct advantage of switching behaviors in enhancing encryption confidentiality.

title: Icarus: High-Altitude Platform Station Navigation with LEO Satellites
authors: Will Barrett, Sharbel Kozhaya, Jennifer Sanderson, Joe Saroufim, Samer Hayek, Zaher M. Kassas
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: The opportunistic exploitation of Ku-band downlink signals from Starlink and OneWeb low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites for high-altitude platform station (HAPS) navigation is demonstrated. Named after the Greek mythological figure Icarus, the HAPS consisted of a weather balloon carrying a radio frequency (RF) platform. The HAPS was launched in July 2024 from the Moriarty Municipal Airport, New Mexico, USA, landing near Mountainair, New Mexico, USA. The HAPS traveled southward a 2D distance of 105.3 km, with a 3D distance of 119.2 km. The HAPS reached a peak altitude of 25.3 km (83,000+ ft) above ground. The design of the high altitude RF data collection platform is described. Received LEO signals are modeled and software-defined receivers (SDRs) are implemented to estimate the Doppler frequency shift from downlink signals. Extensive experimental results are presented analyzing Starlink and OneWeb downlink signals over four HAPS segments: (i) stationary at ground-level, (ii) initial takeoff, (iii) cruising altitude, and (iv) peak altitude. SDR tracking results are presented comparing the performance during stationary and in-flight scenarios, and the the received carrier-to-noise density ratio (C/N0) is characterized. An extended Kalman filter (EKF) is designed to fuse Starlink and OneWeb Doppler measurements with altimeter data to estimate the HAPS' states, assuming two dynamical models: velocity random walk and Wiener process acceleration, with the latter model yielding more accurate results, namely a 3D position root mean-squared errors (RMSEs) of (i) 306 m over 4.12 km during initial ascent, (ii) 729 m over 28.24 km while traveling through a presumed jet stream, and (iii) as accurate as 17 m at peak altitude over a 1.38 km trajectory.

title: Coupled Half-Bridge LLC Resonant Converter With Reduced Switches and Current Sharing Ability
authors: Xinhui Zhao, Hongliang Wang, Zongheng Wu, Lingling Zhang, Mingyang Han, Yan-Fei Liu, Xiumei Yue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: A novel coupled half-bridge LLC resonant converter is proposed in this paper, which has fewer switches and automatic current sharing ability compared with the traditional parallel half-bridge LLC resonant converter. Since the two resonant networks in the proposed converter share the same power switches, the number of devices is reduced by half, which is beneficial for increasing system power density. In addition, the resonant inductor is shared by the two resonant networks, which means that the uneven current in the two LLC resonant converters, caused by differences in resonant parameters, can be shared. The operation mode of the proposed converter is analyzed, and it has the same characteristics as the traditional LLC resonant converter. Besides, the coupled resonant network is analyzed on the basis of Fundamental Harmonic Analysis (FHA), and the current sharing effect of the proposed converter is analyzed. Further, a 1kW experimental platform is built, and the theoretical analysis and simulation results are validated by experimental results.

title: Wholesale Market Participation via Competitive DER Aggregation
authors: Cong Chen, Ahmed S. Alahmed, Timothy D. Mount, Lang Tong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: We consider the aggregation of distributed energy resources (DERs), such as solar PV, energy storage, and flexible loads, by a profit-seeking aggregator participating directly in the wholesale market under distribution network access constraints. We propose a competitive DER aggregator (DERA) model that directly controls local DERs to maximize its profits, while ensuring each aggregated customer gains a surplus higher than their surplus under the regulated retail tariff. The DERA participates in the wholesale electricity market as virtual storage with optimized generation offers and consumption bids derived from the proposed competitive aggregation model. Also derived are DERA's bid curves for the distribution network access and DERA's profitability when competing with the regulated retail tariff. We show that, with the same distribution network access, the proposed DERA's wholesale market participation achieves the same welfare-maximizing outcome as when its customers participate directly in the wholesale market. Extensive numerical studies compare the proposed DERA with existing methods in terms of customer surplus and DERA profit. We empirically evaluate how many DERAs can survive in the competition at the long-run equilibrium, and assess the impacts of DER adoption levels and distribution network access on short-run operations.

title: Congruent kinesthetic and tactile haptic rendering accelerates the learning of highly dynamic virtual sensorimotor tasks_supp2-3698607.mp4
authors: L. ALEXANDRE RATSCHAT
publish: N/A

title: A 144- μW Phase-Interval Balanced RVCO With 142% Tuning Range and 218.2-dBc/Hz FoM TA
authors: Haochuan Wang, Tingwei Gong, Chenglong Zhu, Wenkai Jin, Guodong Su, Mang I. Vai, Sio Hang Pun, Yuhua Cheng, Guo Qing Luo, Hui Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a phase-interval balanced ring voltage-controlled oscillator (RVCO) with ultra-low power and ultra-wide frequency tuning range (FTR), for wearable or implanted brain-computer sensors. An approximate phase-interval error analysis model is developed to quantify the phase imbalance introduced by switched capacitors in phase-combined RVCOs. Based on this model, digitally controlled switched capacitors are incorporated into the low-power RVCO core to significantly expand the FTR while reducing phase-interval imbalance. An enhanced time-window phase combiner (E-TWPC) is further proposed to improve tolerance to residual phase-interval errors and mitigate phase noise degradation over a wide operating range. Prototyped in 65-nm CMOS, the proposed oscillator consumes only 16-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$144~\mu{W}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and achieves an FTR of 440MHz-2.6GHz (142%) under 4-bit digital control. This RVCO maintains comparable phase noise performance to existing RVCOs, while occupying a compact area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.00057~mm^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, achieving a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{FoM}_{TA}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 218.2dBc/Hz at 1MHz offset.

title: FedTransformer-Edge: A Unified Framework for Resource-Adaptive Federated Transformer Learning with Cross-Modal Attention in Heterogeneous IIoT Networks
authors: Iftikhar Rasheed, Hala Mostafa, Muhammad Adeel, Saad Alahmari, Saad Nasser AlTamimi
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Deploying transformer-based anomaly detection in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) networks poses unique challenges including stringent latency requirements, heterogeneous protocols, and safety-critical operations. The proposed work combines multimodal federated learning with adaptive resource optimization, specifically designed for diverse edge devices by addressing three key challenges: (1) a Cross-Modal Progressive Attention (CMPA) mechanism that enables the efficient fusion of vibration, temperature, and pressure sensor data through trainable routing matrices, thus, reducing the complexity from O(T2max) to O(Tmax log Tmax); (2) a Resource-Adaptive Federated Optimization (RAFO) algorithm with provable convergence guarantees under non-IID and asynchronous conditions; and (3) an adaptive differential privacy scheme that dynamically adjusts privacy budgets based on model convergence and data sensitivity. The theoretical analysis confirms that the convergence bounds under asynchronous updates and privacy composition is guaranteed. Evaluation is performed on four IIoT datasets (TON-IoT, SWAT, WADI, and HAI) that demonstrates that FedTransformer-Edge attains 91.3% F1-score (±2.1%, 95% CI) while reducing communication overhead by 62% and energy consumption by 48% compared to other state-of-the-art base-lines. The framework maintains real-time performance (3.7ms average latency) on resource-constrained devices with 512MB RAM, validated through deployment on 50 heterogeneous edge devices.

title: Modeling, Simulation, and Prototype Fabrication of a Saturable-Core Reactor
authors: Ábner C. P. Pacheco, Ivan N. Santos, Elise Saraiva, Guilherme D. Da C. Oliveira, Paulo H. O. Rezende, Gustavo T. A. Da Silva, José A. De S. Brito
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents the complete development of a 500 kVAr saturable-core reactor prototype, including design, computational modeling, fabrication and laboratory testing. The design was based on classical electromagnetic‐circuit principles, and the resulting data were used by TRAEL Transformers to manufacture the equipment and were implemented computationally in ANSYS. Laboratory tests were conducted to assess its operation and effectiveness; among these, the power–voltage characteristic was used to compare experimental results with those from simulation. The analysis demonstrates that the design exhibits robust performance for power-system applications.

title: Pretrained Model Recommendation for Downstream Fine-tuning
authors: Jiameng Bai, Sai Wu, Jie Song, Junbo Zhao, Gang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: As a fundamental problem in transfer learning, model selection aims to rank off-the-shelf pretrained models and select the most suitable one for the new target task. Existing model selection techniques are often constrained in their scope and tend to overlook the nuanced relationships between models and tasks. In this paper, we present a pragmatic framework Fennec, delving into a diverse, large-scale model repository while meticulously considering the intricate connections between tasks and models. The key insight is to map all models and historical tasks into a transfer-related subspace, where the distance between model vectors and task vectors represents the magnitude of transferability. A large vision model, as a proxy, infers a new task's representation in the transfer space, thereby circumventing the computational burden of extensive forward passes and reliance on labels. We also investigate the impact of the inherent inductive bias of models on transfer results and propose a novel method called archi2vec to encode the intricate structures of models. The transfer score is computed through straightforward vector arithmetic with a constant time complexity of O(k) per model inference (where k is the dimension of the latent space). Finally, we make a substantial contribution to the field by releasing a comprehensive benchmark that includes 105 different models. We validate the effectiveness of Fennec through rigorous testing on two benchmarks. Our framework achieves the best evaluation accuracy on both benchmarks. On the PARC benchmark, the feature extraction time is 6.5× faster than the baseline at the same level, and the inference time is 5.7× faster than the fastest baseline. On the larger benchmark, the performance gains are even more pronounced, with feature extraction time being 52.3× faster and inference time 1.8× faster than the fastest baseline, demonstrating remarkable performance efficiency. The code has been made publicly available at: https://github.com/Fay-why/Fennec.

title: A Soft Worm Robot for Sub-Centimeter Scale Pipeline Inspection_supp1-3699247.mp4
authors: Yitong Zhou
publish: N/A
abstract: Inspection of sub-centimeter pipelines with continuous spatial curvatures remains challenging due to extreme geo metric confinement. This paper presents an ultralight, trunkless worm-inspired robot actuated by a single twisted and coiled actuator (TCA) specifically optimized for short-range inspection within localized extreme spatial bottlenecks. By integrating actuation and structural support into a single TCA and eliminating rigid trunks and auxiliary recovery mechanisms, the robot achieves a compact and lightweight design, with a mass of 0.5–1.1 g, a length of 32 mm, and a diameter of 4–10 mm. Directional peristaltic locomotion is enabled by a wedge-based micro silicone frustum foot that provides passive frictional anisotropy without complex control. A simplified mechanics-based anchoring model captures the dominant directional friction asymmetry induced by foot geometry and guides parameter optimization. Experiments demonstrate a maximum friction anisotropy ratio of 4.75, enabling stable crawling at 0.40 mm/s horizontally and 0.13 mm/s vertically in 4–9 mm pipelines, including complex three dimensional curved configurations. Blind inspection experiments with an onboard micro-camera further validate the robot's potential for inspection in highly confined pipeline environments.

title: Robust Rigid Body Pose Estimation for Sparse Point Sets Under Heteroscedastic Noise
authors: Hao Wang, Taogang Hou, Tianmiao Wang, Buhui Jiang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Rigid body pose estimation from sparse point correspondences is important in robotics but remains challenging when measurements are affected by heteroscedastic noise and occasional outliers. This issue is particularly evident in marker-based tracking of fast-moving platforms, where localization uncertainty may vary substantially across markers due to motion blur, occlusion, and sensing artifacts. To address this setting, we develop a robust registration framework tailored to sparse and structured point sets by modeling residuals with a heavy-tailed Student's t-distribution. Using its Gaussian scale mixture representation, the estimation problem is formulated as a maximum-likelihood objective with latent correspondence reliabilities, and solved via an iteratively reweighted least-squares procedure with a graduated non-convexity schedule. The resulting method adaptively down-weights unreliable measurements while retaining the efficiency of weighted rigid alignment. Simulation results across multiple noise models and outlier ratios show that the proposed approach achieves consistently low rotation and translation errors in sparse regimes. Real-world experiments on high-speed quadrotor tracking further demonstrate improved geometric consistency and reduced trajectory jerk relative to standard least-squares estimation.

title: Reconstructing Temporal Heterogeneity: A Multi-domain Collaborative Analysis Framework for Robust Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Hengrui Li, Wenxue Cui, Yifeng Wang, Chunshan Dong, Wenju Li, Tianyi Lu, Jiangpeng Shi, Jianfeng Wang, Yongbing Zhang, Shaohui Liu
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Real-world time series data typically exhibit multi-scale patterns, heterogeneous structures, and non-stationarity. Capturing these multifaceted temporal variations remains a fundamental yet challenging problem in time-series forecasting. Despite considerable advancements in recent years, most existing approaches still depend on conventional decomposition paradigms that separate time series into trend and seasonal components, while neglecting abrupt events, and systemic perturbations in practical applications. This limitation not only introduces forecasting bias but also hinders model generalization and interpretability in complex environments. To tackle these difficulties, we propose a Multi-domain Collaborative Analysis Framework (MCAF). The framework addresses temporal heterogeneity through structural decoupling and domain-specific modeling. Specifically, each time series is decomposed into three distinct components: trend, seasonal, and residual. These components are then processed through specialized modeling pathways, each tailored to capture their unique dynamics in either the time, frequency, or time-frequency domain. A unified fusion mechanism subsequently integrates the extracted multi-domain representations. Extensive experiments show that MCAF consistently delivers high accuracy and robustness across diverse forecasting horizons.

title: An Actively Controlled Quasi-Resonant Excitation Topology for High-Resolution Transient Electromagnetic Sensors
authors: Tao Guo, Zhenzhu Xi, He Wang, Sheng Zhou, Yinlong Xu, Jiaming Sun, Wei Liu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: The transient electromagnetic (TEM) method relies on acquiring weak secondary magnetic fields for subsurface sensing. However, the highly inductive nature of the transmitting sensor coil inherently prevents an instantaneous turn-off of the excitation current. This non-ideal trailing effect generates a primary field interference that heavily contaminates early-time secondary signals, creating a severe shallow detection blind zone. Even when spatial coil topologies are employed to offset the primary field, early-time resolution remains strictly constrained by the transient performance of the current falling edge. To address this sensor-level bottleneck, this article proposes a novel quasi-resonant fast turn-off circuit designed to optimize the excitation waveform for TEM systems. By integrating a series resonant capacitor and an active damping network, the proposed topology achieves rapid current transition without requiring bulky energy storage, thereby preserving instrument portability. Experimental and simulation results indicate that the circuit effectively completes the turn-off process of an 8.8 A excitation current within 20 μs while maintaining excellent linearity. Furthermore, breaking through the constraints of passive snubber circuits, the turn-off duration can be flexibly extended via active control logic to meet specific shallow-detection requirements. Comparative analysis with an energy-dissipation quasi-resonant rectifying circuit (EDQRRC) demonstrates that the proposed method significantly shortens the turn-off duration and suppresses post-turn-off parasitic oscillations. This hardware advancement effectively minimizes the shallow detection blind zone, providing robust support for the acquisition of high-fidelity early-time secondary field data.

title: LCSM: A Local Chart-based Statistical Model for Femoral Shape Analysis
authors: Lujian Zhang, Tianyu Fu, Dejin Yang, Jingyi Liu, Jiaju Zhang, Deqiang Xiao, Jingfan Fan, Danni Ai, Hong Song, Yixin Zhou, Jian Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Statistical shape modeling of the femur is vital for clinical applications, yet linear methods struggle with complex geometric variations. While recent chart-based generative models like ChartPointFlow excel at general point cloud reconstruction, their direct application to the nuanced domain of SSM is limited. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel framework that adapts and significantly extends the chart-based paradigm for robust statistical shape analysis. Our core contributions are threefold. First, we are applying this substructure-based generative approach to the statistical modeling of anatomical shapes, moving beyond reconstruction. Second, to overcome the limitations of baseline encoders in capturing intricate anatomical details, we design a Geometric-enhanced Dynamic Graph Convolutional encoder that integrates multi-level geometric feature encoding. Third, to better learn the statistical distribution across a population, we introduce a point-wise feature augmentation module, termed Fine Feature Mixture module, which enriches feature diversity and robustness by fusing fine-grained representations across different samples. Extensive reconstruction and generation experiments on a femur dataset demonstrate our framework’s superiority. Furthermore, evaluations on multiple ShapeNet categories validate its generalization and applicability to diverse 3D shape modeling tasks. Code will be available in https://github.com/LujZhang/LCSM.

title: Evaluating the Generalization Ability of an Ensemble-Based Fake News Detection Model across Multiple Domains
authors: Jyoti, Yogesh Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: In order to be put in practice, systems should be able to generalize beyond their training datasets on which they have been trained. This paper investigates the cross-domain generalizability of an NDetect fake news detection ensemble stacking model. The framework integrates various machine learning classifiers in a meta-learning fashion, trained on the ISOT dataset with TF-IDF text representations. Under the premise of being able to evaluate the trained model directly without fine-tuning across four individual heterogeneous benchmark datasets LIAR, FakeNewsNet, CoAID, and PHEME for domain shift robustness. The experimental results demonstrate strong in-domain performance 0.89 on ISOT with consistent yet diminished performance across unseen domains, thereby underlining some benefits and limitations of ensemble learning over distributional shift. These findings underscore the importance of an explicit cross-dataset evaluation paradigm when assessing different classes of fake news detectors.

title: A Predictive Machine Learning Framework for Crane Component Fatigue Under Cyclic Loads
authors: Md Sahin Ali, Md Morsalin Islam, Durjoy Dev Sharma, Qi Zhang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This paper presents a predictive machine learning (ML) framework for real-time fatigue assessment of crane components under cyclic loads. Conventional physics-based models, relying on S-N curves and Miner’s rule, are computationally intensive and poorly suited to variable operating conditions. Our data-driven framework overcomes these limitations by learning nonlinear relationships between operational telemetry-hoist load, boom kinematics, and environmental factors-and fatigue damage accumulation. We evaluate ensemble and recurrent neural network architectures, with an optimized Gradient Boosting model achieving the best predictive performance in predicting damage-equivalent stress compared to traditional methods. Model interpretability further reveals high-risk operational regimes, such as lifts at critical boom angles under wind loads. This framework supports the transition from scheduled to predictive maintenance, supporting real-time health monitoring and remaining useful life (RUL) estimation. The results highlight the potential to enhance safety, optimize maintenance schedules, and extend the service life of critical crane assets through actionable, data-driven insights.

title: Moringa Leaf Quality Grading Prediction using Deep Learning
authors: Asina Begum, Ajeeth Kumar, Mohammed Rahees V A, Jithin Rajan, Nanda Gopan K, Basil Varkey
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Moringa leaves are known to have excellent nutritional and treatment value worldwide, although manual quality measurement is still labor intensive, inconsistent, and is prone to mistakes especially when large quantities of leaves have to be achieved. In this paper, a study is suggested to classify Moringa leaves into export quality, value-added, and reject grades based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and is automated. Online repositories and own captures provided a complete set of images which was pre-processed to grayscale, resized, and removed noise in addition to adding artificial images to enhance the strength of the models. The CNN model is composed of several convolutional and pooling layers that have ReLU activation and a final Softmax classifier. With experimental findings, there are better accuracy, reliability and scalability than the manual inspection which is traditional. The system provides a realistic, real-time aid to farmers and exporters improving quality control and export readiness besides minimizing manpower and error.

title: Analysis of HEXAD player types and GUESS user experience in FPS game case studies: Valorant, Counter Strike 2, and Rainbow Six Siege
authors: Jevon Brian Adriel, Dyah Wahyu Sukmaningsih
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Research has proven that satisfaction can lead to player loyalty and their tendency to continue playing. However, there is a gap in understanding the relationship between player motivation to play and their preference in terms of satisfaction. Therefore this study focuses on how various kinds of player motivation can lead to their overall satisfaction in gaming. The research is using a quantitative survey method where more than 150 responders who are actively playing competitive First Person Shooter (FPS) games such as Valorant, Counter 2, and Rainbow Six Siege participated in the research. The study used the HEXAD model to identify each player's motivation and used the GUESS scale to measure their satisfaction with the system. The result of the study shows some significant relationships between player satisfaction and several types of player motivation which overall the study suggest that even though FPS players are mostly driven by rewards and ranking progression, their natural satisfaction on FPS games are influenced by social interactions between players.

title: A Comprehensive Comparison of Vanilla GAN, WGAN, and DCGAN Variations
authors: Yogita Yashveer Raghav, Prashanth Kumar Kata, Sarita, Sangita Choudhary, Dhawani Nanda
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: Generative adversarial network is the most popular neural network among all neural networks which comes under unsupervised learning. It is a special network due to its architecture and loss function. It has wide applications in the fields like audio, video, data generation, image processing, text to speech conversion, image translation etc. A lot of GANs are introduced to do so. Few of them are Cycle GAN, MolGAN, Progressive GAN, Conditional GAN etc. These are known as variations of GAN. GAN is famous for its high quality and diverse image generation. But there are many GAN variations which create confusion which to use for image generation. This paper explains the idea behind GAN and compares its two variations which are DCGAN and WGAN on various parameters like computation time, image quality, image diversity etc. In the experiment, it is found that WGAN is the most suitable GAN for image generation and must be used over DCGAN and vanilla GAN.

title: Explainable Employee Attrition Prediction using PCA-Optimized Random Forest and SHAP
authors: T. Padma, A.S. Kanishik, S. Dinesh
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Employee attrition is one of the most serious challenges in the organization because of its recruitment cost, loss of talent, and reduction in productivity. In this research, the Employee Attrition Prediction System has been developed using the Random Forest Machine Learning Algorithm. In this work, the dimensional reduction of the dataset has been carried out using the PCA technique in order to make the system efficient. The system also uses the SHAP technique for the interpretation of the results using the Random Forest Algorithm. Based on the historical data of employees such as age, department, role, years of experience, salary, and performance, the system determines whether the employees will stay in the organization or will attrite. Data preprocessing for high-quality data includes feature encoding, management of missing values, and removal of unnecessary features. After that, EDA is carried out for the understanding of the data distribution, pattern, and trend. This helps in highlighting the key reasons for employee attrition and aids in decision-making by the management. In addition to performing exploratory data analysis to detect trends and factors impacting employee attrition, feature engineering and feature importance analysis will be conducted to identify the most significant factors leading to attrition, including overtime hours, average monthly earnings, and total years worked. To ensure predictive accuracy and reliability, a Random Forest Model was tuned using hyperparameter optimization techniques such as RandomizedSearchCV and GridSearchCV to achieve the optimal model.

title: Effect of Surface Shape on Electric Contact Resistance for High-Current Copper Conductors
authors: Francesco Rasetti, Giuliano Bissacco
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Production Technologies and Systems for E-Mobility (EPTS)
abstract: Hairpin winding technology has established itself as an industrial standard in EV motors production. Among the main advantages of hairpins over classic winding technology are higher copper fill factor and predictable wire position, making it possible to improve relevant performance indicators for electric motors, such as torque and power density. The final steps of assembly of hairpin arrays, namely twisting and connecting of the conductors, are the most critical towards the final performance of the motor. The deterministic position of hairpins in lamination slots opens new possibilities for the assembly of the electromagnetic circuit, among others the possibility to have reversible connections between the wires. Reversible winding connections could allow easier assembly process chain, better maintainability and reusability of the different parts of the winding assembly. The main obstacle for reversible connections to provide a valid alternative to laser welding is the onset, upon separation of the circuit into separate parts, of electric contact resistance (ECR). ECR describes the sum of the resistance components introduced when multiple parts of a circuit are connected. Contact resistance is a complex phenomenon, and it is influenced by many factors, including contact pressure, temperature and surface conditions. The objective of this paper is to describe, through a combination of numerical and experimental findings, the main factors affecting ECR in hairpin wire connections, to understand the marginal impact of such factors on the generated resistance, and to present possible strategies for minimization of ECR in motor windings by optimizing conductors’ process chain.

title: Quantum-Enhanced Hybrid Variational Neural Network for IoT Intrusion Detection
authors: Aayush Shrivastava, Nikhat Raza Khan, Ashish Singhal, Lalit Kumar, Amit Bindal, Sameeksha Verma
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Weaknesses arising from heterogeneous device structure, high-dimensional traffic patterns, and more complex nonlinear attack behaviors. The traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS), which rely on the classical machine learning models, often face the challenges of scalability, feature explosion, and low representational ability when handling large-scale IoT traffic data. To meet these challenges, we propose a Hybrid Quantum-Classical Variational Neural Network (HQCVNN) for effective intrusion detection in IoT systems. The model utilizes a Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) as a nonlinear quantum feature map followed by dense neural layers for overall classification. Angle embedding with entanglement layers enables the encoding of quantum data and leads to a better separability of features in high-dimensional space, whilst the classical optimization guarantees efficient training on NISQ time scales. The model is implemented using Qiskit and evaluated on classical benchmark intrusion detection datasets. Experimental results show the proposed method has better classification accuracy, F1-score, and complexity reduction than conventional ANNs and SVMs. The results demonstrate the promise of hybrid quantum machine learning systems with end-to-end general-purpose capabilities in future IoT security and provide pragmatic impressions on how to deploy them on near-term quantum processors.

title: Beamforming Enhancement Using Unipolar Directivity of Pressure-Gradient Vector Hydrophone
authors: Zan Li, Xinjing Huang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Beamforming with scalar hydrophone linear arrays suffers from port-starboard ambiguity, and grating lobes emerge when the element spacing exceeds half a wavelength, which impairs the detection and localization of acoustic targets. A beamforming enhancement method is proposed based on the unipolar directivity of pressure-gradient vector hydrophones, which can suppress acoustic source interference from undesired directions and eliminate port-starboard ambiguity. The receiving model of a two-dimensional pressure-gradient vector hydrophone is established, and two derived quantities with unipolar directivities are constructed using only the sound pressure collected by each channel. By analyzing the receiving theory of linear arrays, it is demonstrated that the unipolar directivity can effectively suppress grating lobes and enable selective reception of targets. A microphone cluster is used to simulate a pressuregradient vector hydrophone in air, to test its directivity and suppression effect on interference (grating lobes) from undesired directions during beamforming. The experimental results show that the directivities of the derived quantities are consistent with the theoretical results, and the main lobe intensities of incident waves from $70^{\circ}, 47^{\circ}, 33^{\circ}$ and 21° are suppressed to $48.3 \%, 9.3 \%$, 6.2% and 2.6% of that of the incident wave from 93°, respectively. This method provides a solution for the anti-interference detection and localization of acoustic sources with large apertures and short wavelengths.

title: Does Bullying Harm Academic Achievement? Causal Machine Learning Evidence from Moroccan Students
authors: Ibrahim Allali, Mohammed Bijou, Lahboub Zouiri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study examines the causal effect of bullying exposure on students’ mathematics performance in Morocco using PISA 2022 data and the Generalized Random Forest (GRF) framework in a causal forest setting. Mathematics achievement is measured by the standardized PISA math score, while bullying is captured by the continuous index BULLIED, constructed from nine victimization-frequency items and scaled using an IRTbased procedure. To address confounding, we condition on a rich set of student, school, and socioeconomic covariates. The estimated average treatment effect indicates that a one-standard deviation increase in the bullying index, as defined by PISA, leads-ceteris paribus and conditional on observed individual and school characteristics-to an average decline of about 2.6 points in mathematics scores, revealing a statistically significant negative impact. Beyond the mean effect, the distribution of conditional average treatment effects shows substantial heterogeneity, suggesting that some students face markedly larger academic penalties from bullying exposure than others. Robustness checks support the credibility of the estimates: trimming to strengthen common support yields a similar negative effect (ATE $=-2.74$, $\mathrm{SE}=0.56$), a placebo test based on randomizing the bullying index produces a near-zero and insignificant effect, and sensitivity analyses confirm stability across model configurations and sample fractions. Overall, the findings provide causal machine learning evidence that bullying is a meaningful and unevenly distributed barrier to academic performance in Morocco.

title: Pulse Repetition Frequency Tuned Low-Intensity Focused Ultrasound Neuromodulation for Tibial Nerve Targeted Bladder Function Modulation
authors: Young-Soo Lim, Hyunhee Kim, Chulhong Kim, Sung-Min Park
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: This study aims to investigate whether the neuromodulatory efficacy of low-intensity focused ultrasound (LIFU) for peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) can be systematically tuned by the temporal stimulation parameter, pulse repetition frequency (PRF). Methods: LIFU-mediated tibial nerve stimulation (LIFU-TNS) was applied with multiple PRF conditions to evaluate PRF-dependent neuromodulatory effects, with micturition inhibition as the primary outcome. Intra-bladder pressure (IBP) was continuously monitored to evaluate changes in micturition intervals. Neural involvement was confirmed using (1) a lidocaine-induced sciatic nerve (tibial branch) block, and the stimulation paradigm was additionally evaluated in (2) an acetic acid–induced overactive bladder model and (3) freely moving, unconstrained animals to assess robustness across physiological and behavioral conditions. Results: A distinct PRF-dependent neuromodulation was observed, where 100 Hz stimulation elicited the most potent inhibition of micturition, whereas 10 Hz and 1 kHz showed comparatively less effective results. Quantitative analysis of normalized micturition interval (post-stimulation / pre stimulation baseline) confirmed this trend. (10 Hz: 0.98±0.16; 100 Hz: 1.72±0.81; 1 kHz: 0.95±0.35). Importantly, consistent inhibition was replicated across the nerve-block, overactive bladder, and freely moving models, indicating robustness of the effect under diverse physiological and behavioral conditions. Conclusion: The clear PRF-dependent modulation observed in LIFU-TNS establishes PRF as a feasible and tunable control parameter and highlights its potential for guiding precise neuromodulation. Significance: Unlike conventional intensity dependent strategies, this study demonstrates that PRF is essential for effective ultrasound neuromodulation in overactive bladder. These findings establish a systematic parameter-based framework for developing robust peripheral nerve interfaces.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Framework for Adaptive Scaffolding in Large Language Model
authors: Hala El Yabouri, Fatima-Zohra Hibbi, Otman Abdoun
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Adaptivity in systems based on large language Models (LLMs) is a relatively new direction in the research of intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) and user modeling. This paper gives a detailed description of how adaptivity is realized in ITSs that integrate LLMs or LLMs themselves as pedagogical tools. The review aims to answer the question: What are the constitutive dimensions of adaptivity in dialogue-based scaffolding with Large Language Models, and how can these dimensions be formalized into a multi-dimensional framework to guide design and evaluation? We began by defining the concept of adaptivity based on comparison between the implementation of the rigid, rule-based architectures of traditional ITS and probabilistic, generative approaches capable of dynamic adaptation. The field of AIED currently lacks a theoretical framework for conceptualizing adaptivity in LLM-based systems. We address this gap by synthesizing findings from a systematic literature review and coupling them with inductive thematic analysis to develop a comprehensive multi-dimensional framework of adaptivity. We analyze and identify four constitutive dimensions through which LLM-based systems modify their behavior in response to learner needs: Cognitive Adaptivity (modulating informational content to match knowledge states), Strategic Adaptivity (adjusting pedagogical structure and timing of support), Affective Adaptivity (regulating emotional engagement through tone and persona), and Metacognitive Adaptivity (supporting selfregulation and reflective learning processes). All dimensions proposed in the analysis phase are grounded in learning sciences theory, particularly Vygotsky’s Zone of Proximal Development and Wood, Bruner, and Ross’s concept of scaffolding. We also discuss the implications of this shift, including the hallucination trade-off and ethical considerations surrounding overscaffolding. Providing a framework geared toward designers and researchers for designing pedagogically effective LLMbased tutoring systems that move beyond superficial conversational fluency toward strong educational impact.

title: Optimized Cluster Head Selection with Enhanced Energy Efficiency
authors: D. Angeline Ranjithamani, R. S. Rajesh, Christo Ananth
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) consist of numerous sensor nodes that collaboratively monitor environmental and physical conditions. Efficient energy utilization is a critical challenge in WSNs because sensor nodes operate with limited battery power. Cluster-based routing protocols such as Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy (LEACH) are widely used to improve energy efficiency by selecting cluster heads (CHs) that manage communication between sensor nodes and the base station. However, the traditional LEACH protocol does not consider the energy consumption behavior of individual nodes during CH selection. To address this limitation, this paper proposes an optimized cluster head selection method called LEACH-ECB (LEACH based on Energy Consumption Behavior). The proposed model introduces a new parameter known as the Energy Consumption Ratio (ECR), which evaluates the energy usage pattern of each node during communication rounds. Nodes with high ECR values are excluded from future CH selection, ensuring that only energy-efficient nodes participate in cluster leadership. The proposed method was simulated using a network simulator and evaluated using metrics such as energy efficiency, throughput, latency, and number of active nodes. Experimental results demonstrate that LEACH-ECB achieves better energy retention and improved network lifetime compared to the conventional LEACH protocol.

title: Energy-Efficient UAV Delivery Optimization via Clustering and Layered Path Planning
authors: Zhiyuan Li, Shiran Zhou, Nasser Al-Musallami, Abdelhakim Khatab, Claver Diallo
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This paper introduces an innovative framework for optimizing vertical drone delivery systems by integrating clustering techniques and layer creation methodologies. Building on the foundational concepts of energy optimization and operational efficiency highlighted in the existing literature, we propose a comprehensive model that addresses the complexities of last mile logistics in urban environments. Our approach employs clustering to group customers based on proximity and demand, allowing for more efficient routing strategies that minimize energy consumption. In addition, we introduce a layered delivery system that facilitates altitude management and obstacle avoidance to ensure efficient and compliant drone navigation. Through extensive numerical experiments, the model shows significant improvements in delivery efficiency and energy utilization, demonstrating its adaptability to varying payload conditions and environmental constraints. The findings underscore the potential of this framework to advance sustainable urban logistics and inform future research in multi-drone and multidepot delivery systems.

title: The Use of Artificial Intelligence in The Visual Exploration Stage of Children’s Book Character Design
authors: Daffa Rayhan Alghifari, Ferric Limano
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being utilized in visual communication design practices, including the design of characters for children’s books. This study aims to examine the role of AI during the visual exploration stage in the character design process for children’s books, particularly as a tool for reference searching and visual style development. The research employs a qualitative practice-based research approach using the Glass Box method, with a case study focused on the design of characters for a children’s book with a nutrition education theme. Data was collected through design process documentation, reflective observation, and visual analysis of AI-generated explorations and the final design outcomes. The findings indicate that AI can enhance the efficiency of visual exploration and expand the range of character style variations in the early stages of design. Nevertheless, the role of the human designer remains dominant in making conceptual, aesthetic, and narrative decisions. This study affirms that AI functions as a creative support tool rather than a replacement for designers and emphasizes the importance of ethical and contextual integration of AI in children’s book character design.

title: Evaluation and improvement of the thermal performance of power modules using the Finite-Element-Method (FEM) and Multi-Material Additive Manufacturing (MMAM)
authors: Chantal-Liv Lehmann, Daniel Utsch, Mario Sprenger, Nils Thielen, Jörg Franke, Florian Risch, Volker Schulze, Frederik Zanger
publish: 2025 16th International MID Congress Mechatronic Integration Discourse (MID Congress)
abstract: Mechatronic Integrated Devices (MID) for high temperature and harsh environments include power electronic applications. There is a growing need for innovative packaging concepts that enhance thermal performance, and reliability while offering greater manufacturing efficiency. This work proposes a novel approach that combines the evaluation of thermal and thermo-mechanical performance of various innovative designs using numerical simulations with the design freedom of the additive manufacturing technology Vat Photopolymerization (VPP), including an advanced multi-material process with ceramic and metallic slurries. It can be found that the thermal performance is reduced by 16% using aluminum nitride as ceramic material and innovative comprised module designs, which can be demonstrated by thermal simulations and printed by VPP in one single machine.

title: How Social Media Algorithms and Financial Influencer Reach Effect on Investment Decisions of Gen Z
authors: Dedrick Soesanto, Yohannes Kurniawan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study analyzes the influence of financial influencers on Generation Z ‘s investment decisions by considering the role of financial literacy, consumer confidence, and perceived risk. Generation Z was chosen because of the high intensity of social media use and its tendency to use financial influencers as a source of investment information. The research uses a quantitative approach through an online questionnaire survey to Generation $\mathbf{Z}$ in Jakarta who follow financial influencers and have investment interests or experience. The data was analyzed using the Partial Least Squares-Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) method. The results of the study show that financial influencers have a positive and significant influence on Generation Z’s investment decisions. Consumer trust has been proven to mediate this relationship, while financial literacy plays a role as a moderation variable that can reduce less rational investment decision-making. On the other hand, perceived risk has a negative effect on investment decisions. These findings affirm the importance of credibility of financial information and improving financial literacy in the investment decision-making process of Generation $\mathbf{Z}$

title: CuDi: Curve Distillation for Efficient and Controllable Exposure Adjustment
authors: Chongyi Li, Chunle Guo, Ruicheng Feng, Shangchen Zhou, Chen Change Loy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: We present Curve Distillation, CuDi, for efficient and controllable exposure adjustment without the requirement of paired or unpaired data during training. Our method inherits the zero-reference learning and curve-based framework from an effective low-light image enhancement method, Zero-DCE, with further speed up in its inference speed, reduction in its model size, and extension to controllable exposure adjustment. The improved inference speed and lightweight model are achieved through novel curve distillation that approximates the time-consuming iterative operation in the conventional curve-based framework by high-order curve's tangent line. The controllable exposure adjustment is made possible with a new self-supervised spatial exposure control loss that constrains the exposure levels of different spatial regions of the output to be close to the brightness distribution of an exposure map serving as an input condition. Different from most existing methods that can only correct either underexposed or overexposed photos, our approach corrects both underexposed and overexposed photos with a single model. Notably, our approach can additionally adjust the exposure levels of a photo globally or locally with the guidance of an input condition exposure map, which can be pre-defined or manually set in the inference stage. Through extensive experiments, we show that our method is appealing for its fast, robust, and flexible performance, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in real scenes. Project page: https://li-chongyi.github.io/CuDi_files/.

title: Online Coreset Selection for Learning Dynamic Systems
authors: Jingyuan Li, Dawei Shi, Ling Shi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
abstract: With the increasing availability of streaming data in dynamic systems, a critical challenge in data-driven modeling for control is how to efficiently select informative data to characterize system dynamics. In this work, we develop an online coreset selection method for set-membership identification in the presence of process disturbances, improving data efficiency while preserving convergence guarantees. Specifically, we derive a stacked polyhedral representation that over-approximates the feasible parameter set. Based on this representation, we propose a geometric selection criterion that retains a data point only if it induces a sufficient contraction of the feasible set. Theoretically, the feasible-set volume is shown to converge to zero almost surely under persistently exciting data and a tight disturbance bound. When the disturbance bound is mismatched, an explicit Hausdorff-distance bound is derived to quantify the resulting identification error. In addition, an upper bound on the expected coreset size is established and extensions to bounded measurement noise and nonlinear systems are discussed. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated through comprehensive simulation studies.

title: Parameter Estimation of Intermittent Sampling Repeater Jamming Based on Orthogonal Block-Concatenated Waveform Design
authors: Chang Zhou, Wenfeng Chen, Zhu Tang, ALei Chen
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Intermittent Sampling Repeater Jamming (ISRJ) achieves both deception and suppression effects by flexibly setting jamming parameters. Therefore, fast and high-precision estimation of ISRJ parameters is the premise for radar to implement effective anti-jamming. Existing estimation methods based on time-frequency analysis and ambiguity function transformation have problems such as high computational complexity and poor robustness at low jamming-to-noise ratios. To address these issues, this paper proposes a lightweight jamming parameter estimation method based on radar transmitter waveform optimization and receiver-side multi-block energy center calculation. This method uses deep learning to design a set of orthogonal phase-coded block sub-blocks with low autocorrelation sidelobes and low cross-correlation peaks, and splices them into a radar transmit waveform. When affected by ISRJ, based on the peak position offset and amplitude distribution of the matched filtering output of each sub-block, the Multi-block Energy Center Calculation (MEC) algorithm is used to calculate the jamming sampling period and duty cycle, avoiding complex transform domain operations. Simulation results show that Monte Carlo experiments verify the effectiveness of the proposed parameter estimation algorithm, which can provide an engineering implementation path for radar to sense ISRJ.

title: MU-MIA: Machine Unlearning for Membership Inference Attacks
authors: Hongming Yang, Guopu Zhu, Long Sun, Wei Jiang, Xinpeng Zhang, Tao Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The widespread deployment of deep learning models across various applications has raised significant concerns regarding data privacy. Membership inference attacks (MIAs), a major privacy threat, aim to determine whether a specific sample is used during model training, thereby posing significant risks to sensitive information. Most existing MIA methods rely on the model’s final state output, overlooking the process by which the model memorizes training samples. To better exploit model memorization for MIAs, we propose a novel attack method called machine unlearning-based membership inference attack (MU-MIA). The proposed method introduces machine unlearning to incrementally reduce the model’s memorization of specific samples, generating a forgetting trajectory for each sample. The forgetting trajectory is composed of temporal variations in different metrics of the sample during machine unlearning. To distinguish member from non-member samples, we design a BiLSTM-based binary classifier with attention, which captures discriminative temporal patterns within each forgetting trajectory. Moreover, the machine unlearning phase of our attack is conducted under a zero-shot setting, which eliminates the need for any real data during the unlearning process, thereby improving the practicality and generalizability of the attack. We evaluate the proposed MIA method across different datasets and model architectures, and the comparative experimental results show that our method outperforms existing baseline attack methods.

title: Fast and Accurate Beam Direction Calibration Method for Phased Array Antennas Using PCA
authors: Zengdi Bao, Ziwei Peng, Yinbo Zhao, Liang Zhang, Zaiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Various methods have been proposed in the literature to calibrate the amplitudes and phases of phased array channels. However, physical factors—such as mutual coupling between antenna elements, quantization errors in phase shifters, and residual channel calibration inaccuracies—often interact to cause the actual beam direction to deviate from the designed angles. These deviations typically become more pronounced at larger scanning angles. Consequently, accurate beam direction calibration is critical for phased arrays employed in high-precision angle-of-arrival estimation. In this work, we propose a fast and accurate offline calibration method based on principal component analysis (PCA) for phased arrays whose channels have been calibrated. By extracting beam direction error features at a subset of angles and fitting their principal components, our method accurately predicts beam direction errors across all required angles. Experimental results show that, when measuring at only 1/22 of the angles, the three standard deviations (3σ) of the differences between predicted and measured beam directions remain below 0.25º for 12×12-element arrays within a ±25º range in both azimuth and elevation planes. Compared with typical interpolation methods, our approach substantially improves the efficiency of beam direction calibration and the underlying mechanisms are thoroughly analyzed.

title: HBA-VIO: High-Precision Direct Sparse Monocular Visual-Inertial Odometry via Hybrid Bundle Adjustment
authors: Zhiyu Zhou, Han Zhang, Feng Hui, Junyi Wu, Yu Liu
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is a core perception module for autonomous robots, where high-precision pose estimation is essential for reliable navigation. However, low-cost setups equipped with inertial measurement units (IMUs) and monocular cameras suffer from cumulative trajectory drift. In this article, we propose HBA-VIO, a high-precision direct sparse monocular VIO system based on hybrid bundle adjustment (HBA). First, to address initialization challenges in IMU-based systems, a multistage visual-inertial initialization strategy is proposed, consisting of static initialization and coarse-to-fine visual-inertial alignment. This ensures precise calibration between monocular visual odometry and inertial measurements across diverse scenarios. Second, for high-accuracy trajectory estimation, we design an HBA framework integrating backward-decoupled preintegration residuals, inertial-only prior residuals, cross-frame preintegration residuals, and a scale refinement module, which effectively refines the global scale and suppresses cumulative trajectory drift. Extensive evaluations are performed on real-world challenging scenarios. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate that HBA-VIO achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, validating the effectiveness of each technical component and the system’s strong robustness to real-world disturbances.

title: Adaptive Lévy Flight-Based Optimization for Fetal ECG Recovery and Arrhythmia Detection
authors: Shivangi Diwan, Mridu Sahu, Varun Bajaj
publish: IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems
abstract: Early detection of fetal cardiac abnormality facilitates timely intervention and management. Existing noninvasive fetal electrocardiogram (fECG) extraction techniques often rely on fixed or heuristic optimization parameters and segment-level evaluation strategies, limiting robustness under nonstationary maternal–fetal signal overlap and introducing potential information leakage. This work presents an adaptive optimization framework utilizing the adaptive Lévy flight-based cuckoo search (ALCS) method for improved fECG extraction and detection of fetal arrhythmias, contributing to intelligent human–machine systems in obstetric monitoring. The framework employs ALCS as an outer loop optimizer to determine an optimal initialization of the least mean square (LMS) filter coefficients for adaptive noise cancellation (ANC), where a convergence-aware Lévy flight parameter governs the search process. The Lévy parameter <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda$</tex-math></inline-formula> evolves adaptively within <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$[1.5, 2.0]$</tex-math></inline-formula> enabling an effective transition from exploration to exploitation. The proposed optimizer achieves approximately 1.8–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.0\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> faster convergence than standard LMS, reducing the mean-squared error by <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sim$</tex-math></inline-formula>74% to 0.10 within 50 iterations. A comprehensive feature set exceeding 100 descriptors is extracted from fECG signals and evaluated using ensemble classifiers. Ensuring a leakage-safe evaluation protocol, the experiments were conducted using subjectwise grouped cross-validation with foldwise feature selection on the noninvasive fetal ECG arrhythmia database available from PhysioNet. The proposed framework achieves robust arrhythmia detection, attaining 91.2% accuracy and 95.0% area under the curve across unseen subjects. To quantify systematic bias and agreement across evaluation stages, Bland–Altman analysis is employed to provide clinical interpretability beyond aggregate scores. Overall, the results demonstrate that convergence-aware Lévy-based optimization improves ANC performance for fECG analysis, while acknowledging dataset scale and offline evaluation limitations.

title: MG-YOLO: A Lightweight Deep Learning Model for Detecting Blueberry Fruits at Different Maturities
authors: Qin Ling, Cui Kai, Wen Zelong, Zhang Wenyuan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The yield and quality of blueberry cultivation depend heavily on effective fruit harvesting. Accurate and real-time identification of blueberries at various maturity stages is a prerequisite for precise harvesting. Accordingly, a lightweight network named MG-YOLO is developed for blueberry fruit detection in this study, through multiple enhancements on YOLO11-nano. First, the GBC block integrating gated convolutions and Bottleneck convolutions is adopted to improve adaptive feature discrimination ability, especially for densely occluded blueberries. Second, a novel MG-C3k2 module incorporating multi-scale Ghost convolution is proposed to strengthen feature representation while maintaining model efficiency. Finally, IF-CIoU loss, focusing on inner overlapping areas of bounding boxes and the balance between hard and easy samples, is designed to mitigate the class imbalance issue and accelerate bounding box regression, improving localisation precision. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed MG-YOLO attains 85.7% mAP@0.5 and 60.6% mAP@0.5:0.95, representing remarkable improvements of 4.0% and 5.1% over the baseline, respectively. Furthermore, the parameter count is reduced by 12%, and the model exhibits a detection speed of 51 FPS measured on an NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU, indicating its potential for for real-time automated harvesting applications.

title: Maximum Entropy Inverse RL for Cyber Attack Detection in IoT Networks
authors: Mohamed Massaoudi, Maymouna Ez Eddin
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has significantly expanded the attack surface for cyber threats, making effective intrusion detection systems (IDS) critical for network security. Traditional anomaly detection methods often struggle with high-dimensional network traffic data and evolving attack patterns. This paper presents a novel approach for cyber attack detection using Maximum Entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning (MaxEnt IRL). The proposed method learns reward functions from normal network traffic patterns, enabling the identification of anomalous behaviors that deviate from expected normal operations. By leveraging the maximum entropy principle, the approach captures the underlying distribution of normal traffic states without requiring labeled attack samples during training. The study evaluates the performance with multiple attack types, including Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS), Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) poisoning, and Message Queue Telemetry Transport (MQTT) based attacks. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves 91.18% accuracy, outperforming traditional machine learning approaches, deep learning methods, and other RL attack detection techniques. The results highlight the potential of MaxEnt IRL for robust and adaptive cyber attack detection in IoT environments. All the related codes and datasets publicly available at: https://github.com/Mmassaoudi-cell/maxent-irl-iot-ids.

title: Energy-saving Optimization Control of PEMFC systems for Plateau Railway
authors: Shuqi Xie, Qi Li, Liangzhen Yin, Leshan He, Weirong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The harsh environment and high-power and high dynamic traction load along the plateau railway led to severe energy loss, limited output capacity and even stack halt of proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) systems. To this end, an energy-saving optimization control (ESOC) method is proposed to simultaneously enhance energy recovery and output capacity of PEMFC systems. Based on the close energy interaction between the system and environment, a plateau PEMFC system model is established, and an expander is integrated to achieve the conversion of gas waste heat energy. The contradictory characteristics between the energy recovery capacity and output capacity of the system in the plateau environment are analyzed. Because maximum net power and maximum energy recovery cannot be achieved at the same oxygen excess ratio (OER), a dual-objective optimization method is proposed to determine the optimal OER while strictly adhering to safety constraints. To realize rapid response under complex traction loads, the OER is dynamically adjusted through an active disturbance rejection controller. The comparative experiments on the hardware-in-the loop platform show that the proposed ESOC method can effectively ensure the stable operation of PEMFC systems in plateau areas, and achieve energy recovery and output capacity improvement.

title: Core Values for Ethical AI: A Systematic Review of Frameworks, Contexts, and Applications
authors: Hannah Louise Mulvihill, Taís Fernanda Blauth, Oskar Josef Gstrein, Andrej Zwitter
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems increasingly support decisions with significant ethical and societal implications, necessitating systematic approaches to value-aware design and governance. However, it remains unclear which normative values are most consistently emphasized in AI design research and how these priorities vary across domains and research objectives. This study presents a systematic literature review of 46 peer-reviewed publications identified through six major scholarly databases. The analysis distinguishes theoretical and applied contributions and examines Value Sensitive Design (VSD) alongside related value-protection frameworks to identify recurrent value patterns. Results reveal a convergent core set of values including privacy, fairness/equality, transparency/explicability, accountability/responsibility, and autonomy, that dominate the discourse. In contrast, interpretability, safety, and reliability receive comparatively limited attention, while collective goods and ecological sustainability are rarely addressed. Domain-specific differences are evident: safety-critical and embodied systems prioritize safety and interpretability; data-intensive public sector applications emphasize fairness and privacy; and industrial contexts foreground security, control, and responsibility. Furthermore, framework-oriented studies tend to emphasize procedural values, whereas empirical case studies focus on domain-specific risks such as safety and reliability. Across the corpus, a persistent gap is identified between articulated value commitments and mechanisms for post-deployment verification of value realization. Based on the synthesis of 21 recurrent values, this review derives nine illustrative system-level requirements intended to support the design, evaluation, and governance of value-aware AI systems.

title: Performance, Adaptability, and Security in AI-Driven IIoT: A Survey
authors: Uddin Md. Borhan, Arif Raza, Taki Uddin, Mahbubul Islam, A.K.M. Muzahidul Islam, Jie Chen
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) integrates cyber-physical systems, edge-cloud computing, Artificial Intelligence (AI), and advanced connectivity to enable intelligent, data-driven operations across manufacturing, energy, transportation, and healthcare. The convergence of Operational Technology (OT) and Information Technology (IT), together with pervasive AI-driven automation, complicates meeting the joint requirements of performance, adaptability, and security (PAS). This survey adopts an explicitly AI-centric view of PAS and reviews IIoT research spanning deep reinforcement learning (DRL), multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), digital twins (DTs), federated and collaborative learning, graph neural networks (GNNs), blockchain-assisted analytics, and lightweight and post-quantum cryptography. The reviewed approaches are analyzed across device, edge, fog, cloud, and application layers under industrial constraints such as latency, reliability, resource limits, safety requirements, and regulatory compliance. A PAS-centric taxonomy is introduced that links technique families to deployment layers and application domains, and recurring trade-offs among PAS dimensions are summarized. Open challenges are identified in multi-objective optimization, semantic interoperability, lifecycle validation, and governance, with directions outlined toward secure and sustainable Industry 5.0 IIoT ecosystems.

title: Big Data-Driven Monitoring and Risk Early Warning for Mountainous River Waterways
authors: Zhihao Yao
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Mountainous river waterways are characterized by complex terrain, abrupt hydrological variation, and coupled navigation-geology hazards, while conventional monitoring and alarm systems remain constrained by fragmented data, low precision, and delayed response. This study proposes a big-datadriven monitoring and risk early warning framework for mountainous river waterways. A heterogeneous data fusion architecture is first established to integrate hydrological, meteorological, navigation, geological, and dispatch-related streams. A dynamic warning model is then developed using an optimized long short-term memory (LSTM) network to encode temporal dependency, cross-source interaction, and hazard escalation patterns. On this basis, an intelligent platform integrating acquisition, analysis, diagnosis, and warning push is designed for closed-loop operation. A representative Wujiang mountainous waterway reach is selected for validation using three years of measured data. Results show that the optimized model achieves an overall warning accuracy of 92.7%, improving by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 1. 3 {\%}}$</tex> over a conventional threshold-based method. The false alarm rate decreases to 4.1%, and warning latency under extreme hydrological conditions is controlled within 8 s. The framework accurately identifies high-risk shoals, rapid-flow reaches, and landslide-prone banks, demonstrating a practical route toward safer inland navigation governance and smart water transport upgrading.

title: Design and Optimization of a Waveguide Applicator for Cell Cultures Exposure in the FR1 Band of 5G Signals
authors: Stefania Romeo, Olga Zeni, Anna Sannino, Micaela Liberti, Francesca Apollonio, Alessandra Paffi, Maria Rosaria Scarfî
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: This paper presents the design and optimization of a waveguide applicator for exposure of cell cultures to RF-EMF at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 5 ~ G H z}$</tex>, an operative frequency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G}$</tex> technologies. In accordance with the requirements for an RF applicator, numerical dosimetry analysis was carried out to maximize uniformity of SAR distribution in the cell monolayer and the number of samples to be exposed simultaneously.

title: A Vector-Free Global Terrain Analysis Algorithm and its Application: Extraction of Constructible Areas for Navigation in Wild and Extreme Disaster-Stricken Zones
authors: Boyang Guo, Mianqing Zhong
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Aiming at the inefficiency and heavy multi-source data reliance of traditional methods for constructible area extraction in vector-free extreme scenarios (e.g., war, disaster zones), a DEM-only vector-free global terrain analysis algorithm is proposed. The algorithm constructs a directed weighted slope topological graph integrating slope, azimuth and terrain flow direction via DEM preprocessing and regular sampling, then realizes candidate node preliminary screening, terrain-constrained 8-neighborhood DFS connected domain retrieval, and secondary screening by azimuth consistency and area threshold to automatically extract qualified contiguous constructible areas. Lightweight, globally adaptable and reproducible, it abandons auxiliary data and large GIS tools, and can be rapidly applied to vector-free scenarios to provide efficient technical support for emergency engineering terrain decision-making.

title: Subcellular Microdosimetry in a Virtual 3D HaCaT Cell Exposed to 26.5 GHz Millimeter Waves
authors: Sara Fontana, Noemi Dolciotti, Giulia Renna, Anna Sannino, Gianmarco Ronza, Stefania Romeo, Olga Zeni, Maria Rosaria Scarfî, Francesca Apollonio, Micaela Liberti
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: The deployment of fifth-generation (5G) has renewed interest in the assessment of human exposure to radiofrequency electromagnetic fields (RF-EMF). This study presents the development of an advanced virtual 3D HaCaT cell, reconstructed from confocal microscopy images. These models are employed for microdosimetric simulations at 26.5 GHz to evaluate electric field distributions and volumetric power dissipation density at cellular and subcellular levels.

title: Revisiting Deepfake Image Detection: An Analysis of Cross-Dataset Generalization Limits
authors: Charri Meriem, Zaydi Hayat, Assoul Saliha
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: The rapid development of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) has enabled very realistic photorealistic faces to easily be generated in the manipulations, which are widely known as deepfakes, thereby creating significant obstacles for digital forensics to confirm media authenticity. Although high accuracy has been reported by the latest deep learning solutions on standardized evaluation datasets, their robustness to diverse data distributions remains to be challenging tasks for now. The study considers an analysis on the crossdataset evaluation performance on models designed for general image detection, especially focusing on deepfake images, and two models are evaluated in this study because they are very representative in deepfake detection tasks, one is the generic Convolutional Neural Network with ResNet structure, and the other one is designed to detect the artifacts in the images generated by GANs in digital manipulations, which is specifically referred to as the GAN-CNN model. The two models are evaluated in this work based on two publicly accessible deepfake imagery datasets based on intra- and crossdataset testing protocols, according to which significant performance loss has been found in models when they are evaluated in datasets that were not yet seen in the testing tasks, varying to some degrees according to the datasets in question regarding the deepfake detection performance loss due to domain gap, whereas the performance loss can be lessened with the use of manipulation-focused representations by observing the cross-dataset performance improvement based on detection accuracy in the study on the two models.

title: The effects of climate change on the spread of the common bean diseases
authors: Juvénal Havyarimana, Demba Faye, Messikely Anoman, Doudou Dione, Idy Diop
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris) is extensively cultivated across Africa and Latin America, particularly in regions of high and medium altitudes. It represents a major staple food crop in developing countries, owing to its substantial nutritional and agronomic value. Despite its importance, the crop is highly susceptible to both biotic and abiotic stresses, which adversely affect plant growth and overall productivity. Climate change has further exacerbated these constraints through rising temperatures, increased rainfall variability, and a higher frequency of extreme weather events. Such environmental pressures significantly compromise plant health and reduce bean yields. In the context of rapid global population growth, it is imperative to enhance bean production to ensure food security. This study presents a literature review encompassing research conducted between 2014 and 2025, with particular focus on the impact of climate variability on the prevalence and spread of bean diseases. The review also synthesizes proposed strategies for mitigating the effects of climate-related stresses. The findings underscore persistent challenges, notably the insufficiently explored influence of climate change on the dynamics of plant diseases.

title: Influence of Electric Field Strength on the Dielectric Response of Epoxy Resin foe Electrical Insulation
authors: Qian Wang, Ting Lei, Qiulin Chen, Jiahe Yu, Haohan Zhou, Chao Wu
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: Epoxy resin is one of the most widely-used polymer dielectrics for electrical insulation. Polarization properties are vital to the electric field distribution and the heat management of equipment. Polarization of dielectric is usually investigated by dielectric response, while the voltage applied in previous research is much lower to that in practical operation, which affects the universality of conclusions. Therefore, in this research, the dielectric response of typical epoxy resin under different field strengths is measured, which reaches the magnitude of actual operation. The influence of field strength on different micro polarization processes is revealed, which suggests a linear polarization trend. The charges involved in the polarization processes are found to be mainly thermal-activated, which means the predominant factor of polarization performance is temperature. The findings herein would prominently benefit the design and the maintenance of epoxy-based high voltage equipment.

title: Effect of Dipole Length on the Design and Performance of High Permittivity Materials in 7 T MRI
authors: Vincenzo Miranda, Giuseppe Carluccio, Giuseppe Ruello, Riccardo Lattanzi, Daniele Riccio
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: At 7T MRI, radiofrequency field inhomogeneity and limited penetration arise from wavelength shortening in biological tissues.
